title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
blurb: Security researchers linked the Popa botnet, which has forced millions of Android TV boxes to relay traffic for ad fraud and data scraping over four years, to NetNut, a residential-proxy service run by publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies. The case shows how cheap streaming boxes sold under thousands of brand names quietly conscript buyers' home networks into commercial proxy pools that paying customers can route malicious traffic through.

title: CISA warns Fortinet users to secure devices after FortiBleed leak
blurb: CISA urged Fortinet customers to secure their devices after roughly 74,000 firewall and VPN credentials were exposed in a leak dubbed "FortiBleed." Researcher Volodymyr Diachenko found a server holding plaintext passwords for 73,932 firewall URLs spanning 21,632 domains across 194 countries, including Samsung, Mercedes-Benz, Chevron, AT&T, and Toyota, with attackers already using the credentials against internet-facing devices.

title: Midjourney Medical
blurb: Midjourney, the AI image-generation startup, launched Midjourney Medical, a new effort applying its image models to healthcare and clinical imaging. The move marks the company's first expansion beyond consumer creative tools into a regulated vertical, where accuracy and safety requirements are far steeper than in general-purpose image generation.

title: I found 10k GitHub repositories distributing Trojan malware
blurb: A developer discovered roughly 10,000 GitHub repositories silently distributing Trojan malware, each cloning a legitimate project's name, description, and commit history before adding a readme link to a malicious zip archive. The repos share a detectable pattern of periodically deleting and re-pushing the same commit to refresh the download link, yet GitHub support took nearly two months to remove the ones reported.

title: Google Gemini co-lead Noam Shazeer leaves for OpenAI
blurb: Noam Shazeer, Google vice president of engineering and a co-lead of its Gemini models, announced he is leaving to join OpenAI less than two years after returning to Google. His exit follows the 2024 deal that brought him back from Character.AI and lands as OpenAI, fresh off a confidential IPO filing, intensifies the industry-wide fight for top AI talent.

title: Zero-Touch OAuth for MCP
blurb: The Model Context Protocol's Enterprise-Managed Authorization extension is now stable, letting organizations centrally control MCP server access through their existing identity provider instead of per-user OAuth. Employees get zero-touch setup where needed servers connect on first login, with the client exchanging an ID-JAG token from the IdP for server access tokens. Anthropic, Microsoft, and Okta are among early adopters.

title: Improving health intelligence in ChatGPT
blurb: OpenAI released GPT‑5.5 Instant, which it says brings frontier health intelligence to ChatGPT's free tier, performing comparably to its Thinking models on the company's hardest health evaluations. The improvements, shaped by a global physician network that defines ideal responses and failure modes, target recognizing when urgent care is needed, asking for context, and explaining uncertainty, reaching the more than 230 million people who use ChatGPT for health questions weekly.

title: Embedding Forbidden Text in Spyware to Discourage AI Analysis
blurb: A malware developer is prepending fake system instructions about nuclear and biological weapons to spyware, hiding the text in a JavaScript comment so it never executes but does reach any LLM fed the start of the file. The trick targets naive AI-first triage pipelines that don't isolate untrusted input, potentially triggering refusals or misclassification, though YARA rules, entropy checks, and behavioral analysis still catch the payload.

title: Datasette Apps: Host custom HTML applications inside Datasette
blurb: Simon Willison released datasette-apps, a plugin that hosts self-contained HTML+JavaScript applications inside Datasette, running them in a tightly constrained iframe sandbox that can issue read-only SQL queries (and writes through configured stored queries). The sandbox blocks cookie and localStorage access and injects a CSP header preventing outbound HTTP requests, so a buggy or malicious app cannot exfiltrate private data.

title: Noam Shazeer Joins OpenAI
blurb: Noam Shazeer is joining OpenAI, leaving Google where he co-led the Gemini effort and earlier invented the Transformer architecture. His move adds one of the field's most influential researchers to OpenAI and marks another high-profile departure from Google's AI ranks.

title: AMD silently removes memory encryption from consumer Ryzen CPUs
blurb: AMD has quietly removed Transparent Secure Memory Encryption (TSME) from consumer Ryzen CPUs outside its Pro lineup, leaving users exposed to physical attacks that siphon data from memory chips. The change surfaced after AGESA 1.2.7.0 firmware, is undetectable on Windows, and AMD has neither confirmed whether it was intentional nor explained why engineers cut off discussion.

title: AutoJack Attack Lets One Web Page Hijack AI Agent for Host Code Execution
blurb: Microsoft researchers detailed AutoJack, an exploit chain that turns an AI browsing agent into a path to remote code execution by steering it to a malicious web page whose JavaScript reaches a privileged local service and spawns a host process. The flaw lives in AutoGen Studio's MCP WebSocket handler, which trusted localhost, skipped authentication, and ran request-supplied commands; it never shipped in a stable PyPI release but did reach two unyanked pre-release builds.

title: Police cleans nearly 15,000 SocGholish-infected sites tied to Evil Corp
blurb: Operation Endgame removed SocGholish malware from 14,971 hacked WordPress sites and took down 106 servers tied to Evil Corp.

title: On the Redundancy of Timestep Embeddings in Diffusion Models
blurb: This paper challenges the assumption that diffusion models need explicit timestep embeddings, showing both theoretically and empirically that models can recover the global training optimum without temporal conditioning by inferring noise scales directly from the corrupted input. Ablations on CelebA and CIFAR-10 found the time-agnostic models held structural fidelity and beat their conditioned counterparts on FID, precision, and recall, pointing toward simpler generative architectures.

title: N-Version Programming with Coding Agents
blurb: This paper revisits N-version programming with AI coding agents, testing whether diversity across agent systems, models, and languages produces independent failure modes. Across 48 implementations of the Knight-Leveson Launch Interceptor spec and 1M randomized inputs, the authors find substantial common-mode failure tied to ambiguous spec sections, but majority voting across three-version units cut mean failures from 387 to 131, with thousands of triples showing zero observed failures.

title: Using AI to help physicians diagnose rare genetic diseases affecting children
blurb: OpenAI shared research in which one of its reasoning models helped physicians diagnose rare genetic diseases in children, surfacing 18 new diagnoses across cases that had gone unsolved. The work positions frontier reasoning models as a clinical decision-support tool for specialists, though the diagnoses still require physician review and confirmation before they reach patients.

title: Salesforce Disables Klue App Integration After OAuth Token Abuse Exposes Customer Data
blurb: Salesforce disabled the Klue Battlecards integration after detecting unusual activity that exposed a subset of customer data, the result of an extortion group called Icarus compromising the competitive-intelligence vendor on June 11. Attackers used a leaked legacy credential to steal OAuth tokens connecting Klue to platforms like Salesforce, then accessed connected environments including those of cybersecurity firm Huntress, exposing business contacts and sales data.

title: TypeScript 7.0 RC
blurb: Microsoft released the TypeScript 7.0 Release Candidate, a port of the compiler from JavaScript to Go that runs roughly 10x faster than 6.0 while preserving identical type-checking semantics. Teams at Bloomberg, Figma, Google, and Vercel have already run it on multi-million-line codebases, and developers can install it now via npm install -D typescript@rc.

title: Microsoft discovers new lightweight backdoor that steals cryptocurrency
blurb: Microsoft detected Crypto Clipper, a self-propagating worm that spreads via USB drives and steals cryptocurrency by scanning device clipboards for wallet addresses and seed phrases. The malware exfiltrates stolen credentials and screenshots over Tor through a local SOCKS5 proxy, and pairs the theft with remote code execution, turning a financially motivated stealer into a lightweight backdoor with no traditional installer or IP-based command infrastructure.

title: Microsoft found malware that hijacks crypto wallets and spreads through USB sticks
blurb: Microsoft found malware that hijacks crypto wallets and spreads through infected USB drives, targeting Windows users since February. The worm, identified as Trojan:Win32/CryptoBandits, monitors the clipboard for seed phrases and private keys, exfiltrates them over Tor, and silently swaps in attacker-controlled addresses during transfers.

title: AWS Plugs Coinbase's x402 Into CloudFront, Letting Publishers Charge AI Agents in USDC
blurb: AWS turned on AI traffic monetization inside AWS WAF, letting any site behind CloudFront charge AI agents per request in stablecoins through Coinbase's x402 protocol. The feature returns an HTTP 402 with a price manifest, settles USDC on Base or Solana via Coinbase's Facilitator at no extra cost to CloudFront customers, and marks the first time a hyperscale cloud has wired onchain settlement directly into its content-delivery edge.

title: Project Valhalla, Explained: How a Decade of Work Arrives in JDK 28
blurb: Oracle confirmed that JEP 401: Value Classes and Objects will land in JDK 28, marking the first piece of Project Valhalla to reach the main OpenJDK repository after a decade of work. The change ships as a disabled-by-default preview spanning 197,000 lines across 1,816 files, delivering Valhalla's "codes like a class, works like an int" goal while leaving the rest of the project for future releases.

title: Is it agentic enough? Benchmarking open models on your own tooling
blurb: Hugging Face benchmarks open coding agents on transformers itself, measuring not just correctness but how much work each model spends getting there.

title: Authorities disrupt Evil Corp’s SocGholish botnet
blurb: A global law-enforcement coalition dismantled Evil Corp's SocGholish botnet, seizing 106 servers and remediating nearly 15,000 infected WordPress sites used to seed ransomware.

title: Tesco UK supermarket chain removes 40,000 servers from VMware infrastructure — mass exodus continues due to Broadcom's aggressive subscription model
blurb: Tesco will remove VMware from its fleet of 40,000 servers by 2027, the largest publicly known migration off the platform since Broadcom's 2023 acquisition. The shift follows a 175% VMware price hike and Tesco's $134M lawsuit alleging Broadcom broke perpetual licenses, part of a broader enterprise exodus as Broadcom pushes customers onto pricier subscriptions and pulls support from holdouts.

title: NAMESAKES: Probing Identity Memorization in Text-to-Image Models
blurb: This paper introduces a black-box probe that detects whether a text-to-image model has memorized a real person's face from a name prompt, without needing reference photos, training data, or access to model internals. Tested across state-of-the-art T2I models using NAMESAKES, a dataset of over 1,000 public figures spanning many fame levels, the method reliably separates memorized identities from fabricated ones and surfaces differences across model families.

title: Ubiquiti: Enterprise NAS, Built on ZFS
blurb: Ubiquiti released Enterprise NAS (ENAS), a ZFS-based storage platform aimed at replacing licensed, proprietary enterprise storage with private, local infrastructure. The system emphasizes performance, scalability, and simpler management, extending Ubiquiti's hardware playbook from networking into the data-storage market.

title: Apple Patches Beats Studio Buds Flaw Letting Nearby Attackers Spy via Microphone
blurb: Apple patched a high-severity Beats Studio Buds flaw (CVE-2025-20701) that let attackers within Bluetooth range eavesdrop through the microphone before pairing.

title: Early Users of Anthropic Mythos Still Have Access After US Order
blurb: Some early testers of Anthropic's Mythos AI model have kept access to a preview version even after a US government order forced a full shutdown of other deployments. Roughly 200 organizations, including banks and tech firms such as Cisco and Dragos, use Mythos Preview through Project Glasswing, an Anthropic-cleared collective tasked with hunting for cyber vulnerabilities.

title: Accenture to Acquire Majority Stake in Dragos, All of runZero, NetRise in $4.1 Billion OT Cybersecurity Push
blurb: Accenture announced it will take a majority stake in OT security firm Dragos and fully acquire runZero and NetRise in deals valued at roughly $4.1B. The three companies, projected to generate $208M in ARR by June 2026, will operate under Dragos as an independent unit, positioning Accenture against ServiceNow's $7.75B Armis acquisition in industrial cybersecurity.

title: Apparent Psychological Profiles of Large Language Models are Largely a Measurement Artifact
blurb: A new paper argues that the stable "personality" profiles researchers assign to LLMs are mostly measurement artifacts, not real properties of the models. Testing 56 instruction-tuned LLMs on human personality and risk instruments, the authors attribute 81-90% of between-model variation to a directional response bias rather than the traits being measured, and show a model's apparent profile can be manufactured through item selection.

title: There are no instances in ATProto
blurb: Dan Abramov argues that asking "where are the Bluesky instances?" is a category error, because atproto has no instances the way Mastodon does. He frames atproto as a return to the RSS-era split between hosting and aggregation, where your data lives in your own repository and apps are interchangeable projections over the network rather than walled communities you join.

title: StylisticBias: A Few Human Visual Cues Drive Most Social Biases in MLLMs
blurb: StylisticBias holds identity fixed while varying one visual attribute, finding ~15 cues drive 80% of social bias across six MLLMs.

title: John Jumper to join Anthropic
blurb: Nobel laureate John Jumper is leaving Google DeepMind to join Anthropic after nearly nine years, following a planned break to recharge. Jumper led the AlphaFold protein-structure team that won him the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry, and his move adds a marquee scientific researcher to Anthropic as frontier labs compete for top AI talent.

title: Modos Color Monitor Pushes E-Paper Displays Further
blurb: Modos, a two-person startup, launched a Crowd Supply campaign for the Flow, a 13.3-inch color e-paper monitor running 3,200 x 2,400 resolution, touch input, and a 60-Hz refresh rate over a single USB-C connection. The higher specs come from a new open-source display controller called Enchanter, whose Chrontel CH7516 chip enables DisplayPort 1.1 while keeping every part free of NDA-gated documentation.

title: Migrate from OpenClaw
blurb: hermes claw migrate imports an existing OpenClaw setup, including legacy Clawdbot and Moltbot configs, into Nous Research's Hermes agent. The guide details exactly what transfers, from persona, memory, and skills to model providers, MCP servers, and TTS keys, and notes that hermes setup --portal collapses a multi-provider config into one OAuth login spanning 300+ models.

title: Researchers drop checkm8-style BootROM exploit for A12 and A13 iPhones
blurb: Researchers released a checkm8-style BootROM exploit targeting iPhones built on Apple's A12 and A13 chips, the silicon behind the iPhone XS through iPhone 11 lines. Because the flaw sits in unpatchable read-only boot code, Apple cannot fix it in software, giving forensic tools and jailbreakers persistent low-level access on affected devices that requires physical possession to exploit.

title: Waymo recalls about 3,900 robotaxis after some drove into 'freeway construction zones'
blurb: Waymo is recalling about 3,900 robotaxis to fix a software flaw after 13 incidents in which its vehicles drove into freeway construction zones in Phoenix and the San Francisco area. The voluntary recall, Alphabet's second in just over a month, covers cars running Waymo's fifth-generation driving system and arrives as the company prepares first international launches in London and Tokyo this year.

title: SafeSpec: Fast and Safe LLM via Dynamic Reflective Sampling
blurb: SafeSpec adds a lightweight safety head to speculative decoding, cutting attack success rates 15% on Qwen3-32B while keeping a 2.06x speedup.

title: Google workspace threatening to block Firefox access
blurb: Google Workspace began showing Firefox users a warning that they must download Chrome to keep accessing their work accounts, surfacing the prompt on a Business Plus admin login from an up-to-date browser. Google support told the reporting user the message is only a recommendation for admins reaching admin.google.com rather than a block, but said it would not document the behavior publicly.

title: What Do Safety-Aligned LLMs Learn From Mixed Compliance Demonstrations?
blurb: Benign and harmful compliance demonstrations aren't interchangeable when jailbreaking LLMs, with preference optimization the key training stage that blocks benign examples from raising harmful compliance.

title: FIFA Bug Exposes World Cup Streams to Remote Takeover
blurb: An ethical hacker exploited unenforced client-side access controls in FIFA's Microsoft Entra tenant to reach World Cup broadcast and match-management systems.

title: OpenAI is bringing on some big guns in the lead-up to its IPO
blurb: OpenAI hired Transformer co-author Noam Shazeer and former White House AI policy official Dean Ball as it heads toward an IPO. Shazeer leaves Google, where he co-led Gemini after a $2.7B reacquisition of his startup Character AI, while Ball will run a new Strategic Futures team shaping frontier AI policy and internal governance under Chief Strategy Officer Jason Kwon.

title: Apple patches high-severity eavesdropping vulnerability in Beats Studio Buds
blurb: Apple patched a high-severity vulnerability in its Beats Studio Buds that let attackers within Bluetooth range impersonate previously paired devices and eavesdrop through a phone's microphone. The flaw, CVE-2025-20701, rated 8.8 out of 10, stemmed from Airoha Systems chips and is fixed in Firmware Update 1B211, which installs automatically; Jabra, Bose, and JBL have shipped patches for affected models too.

title: Adobe’s new AI assistant could save you hours in Photoshop and Premiere
blurb: Adobe expanded its AI creative agent across Firefly and Creative Cloud, letting users describe a goal in natural language while the assistant executes multi-step tasks across Photoshop, Premiere Pro, Illustrator, InDesign, and Frame.io. The update also adds Firefly tools for brand-kit generation, image-to-video, and storyboarding, positioning the agent as a connective layer across Adobe's apps rather than a single image or text generator.

title: Cisco to Acquire WideField Security to Boost Splunk’s Agentic SOC
blurb: Cisco announced it will acquire identity lifecycle security startup WideField Security to deepen the identity and session intelligence inside Splunk's Agentic SOC. The deal, whose terms were not disclosed, folds WideField's identity discovery, exposure mapping, and live session monitoring into Cisco's data fabric and marks the company's third cybersecurity acquisition of 2026 after Galileo and Astrix Security.

title: Accenture shells out $4.18B on three companies in big industrial cybersecurity push
blurb: Accenture announced it will acquire a majority stake in industrial cybersecurity firm Dragos for $3.25B and buy runZero and NetRise outright, a $4.18B bet on operational technology security. The deals push the consultancy beyond services into OT cybersecurity software for the first time at scale, covering threat detection, asset discovery, and firmware-level supply chain visibility for power grids, pipelines, and factories.

title: npm v12’s Biggest Security Change: From Implicit to Explicit Trust
blurb: npm v12, expected in July 2026, will block three high-risk installation mechanisms by default: lifecycle scripts, Git-repository installs, and remote-URL installs. The shift from implicit to explicit trust targets vectors JFrog ties to roughly 53% of malicious npm attacks over the past year, including the Shai-Hulud campaigns, requiring developers to opt in with allowScripts, --allow-git, and --allow-remote.

title: John Jumper, who won the Nobel Prize "for protein structure prediction", says he is leaving Google DeepMind after nearly nine years to join Anthropic (John Jumper/@johnjumpersci)
blurb: John Jumper, who shared the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for protein structure prediction with AlphaFold, is leaving Google DeepMind after nearly nine years to join Anthropic. The move hands Anthropic one of the most prominent scientists in AI for biology, deepening the talent war between frontier labs as they compete for researchers who can extend models into the sciences.

title: AI inference startup Baseten reportedly raising $1.5B months after its last mega-round
blurb: Baseten is reportedly closing a $1.5B round at a $13B valuation, the Wall Street Journal reports, just five months after a $300M Series E valued it at $5B. The deal is split-priced, with some investors entering at $13B and others at $11B, reflecting the rush of capital into inference startups that route requests to the cheapest capable model.

title: US energy regulator to order grid operators to expedite AI data center applications — says projects should bring their own power or cut usage during high demand
blurb: The U.S. Federal Energy Regulatory Commission is preparing an order requiring grid operators to fast-track AI data center connections, prioritizing projects that bring their own power or curtail demand during peak stress. The move backs Trump's AI Action Plan and comes as data centers strain the grid, with largest operator PJM Interconnection raising power costs 75.5% in an increase largely blamed on AI buildout.

title: GitLab 19.1 released
blurb: GitLab 19.1 shipped on June 18 with general availability of secret false positive detection in the GitLab Duo Agent Platform, which uses AI to assess each critical and high severity secret finding and flag likely false positives directly in the vulnerability report. The release also adds an always-on availability mode that lets administrators enforce GitLab Duo across an entire instance or top-level group, blocking project owners from disabling it for centralized AI governance.

title: Your Mouse and Eyes Secretly Leak Your Preference: LLM Alignment using Implicit Feedback from Users
blurb: A new dataset captures users' mouse trajectories and eye gaze to train an LLM reward model, lifting accuracy from 55% to 64%.

title: AI coding agents may be getting bad instructions from ‘smelly’ config files
blurb: Researchers at Brazil's Federal University of Minas Gerais published the first catalog of "configuration smells" in coding agent files like Agents.md and Claude.md, finding at least one flaw in 91 of 100 popular open-source repositories. The most common are lint leakage (62%), context bloat (42%), and skill leakage (35%), structural issues that bloat context, waste tokens, and make agents like Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex less reliable.

title: Amazon hopes to challenge Nvidia more directly by selling its AI chips
blurb: Amazon is in early talks to sell its homegrown Trainium AI chips to outside companies for use in their own data centers, AWS AI chief Peter DeSantis told Bloomberg. CEO Andy Jassy has pegged the chip business at a ~$50B run rate, a figure that rivals Intel's revenue but still trails Nvidia's $326B, and any external sales would force AWS to ration capacity that already sells out faster than it can produce.

title: Microsoft, Amazon Cloud Services Face Tough EU Antitrust Law
blurb: The European Commission will issue preliminary findings as early as next week designating Amazon Web Services and Microsoft Azure as gatekeepers under the Digital Markets Act, according to people familiar with the probe. A formal designation would subject both cloud providers to interoperability and self-preferencing rules, marking the first time the DMA reaches enterprise cloud infrastructure rather than consumer platforms.

title: Docs: OpenAI burned through $3.7B in Q1, on revenue of $5.7B, and ended the quarter with $73B+ in cash and marketable securities vs. $40B at the end of December (Erin Woo/The Information)
blurb: OpenAI burned through $3.7B in Q1 against $5.7B in revenue, according to documents reviewed by The Information. The company still ended the quarter with more than $73B in cash and marketable securities, up from $40B in December, giving it a deep runway to keep funding compute and model training despite steep operating losses.

title: Before SpaceX IPO, investors in China secretly acquired stakes
blurb: A leaked investor list obtained by ProPublica shows at least a dozen investors with mainland China, Hong Kong, or Russia addresses acquired SpaceX stakes between 2018 and 2021 through a US middleman firm, including a $15M investment tied to a Beijing VC that also backed sanctioned satellite companies. The disclosure lands days after SpaceX, a contractor on sensitive Pentagon work, barred China and Hong Kong investors from its record IPO over regulatory and compliance risks.

title: Google AI Overviews cite self-serving listicles, but recommend competitors 69% of the time
blurb: Lily Ray's study found Google AI Overviews cited brands' own "best" listicles while recommending competitors instead in 69% of B2B software queries.

title: Trump says he no longer views Anthropic as a national security threat after G7 meeting with CEO
blurb: Trump told Axios he no longer considers Anthropic a national security threat and may ease restrictions on its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models, reversing a three-month standoff with the company. The shift follows a G7 meeting with CEO Dario Amodei and comes days after the Commerce Department ordered Anthropic to seek US approval before foreign nationals could access its most powerful models.

title: [AINews] GLM > GPT? GLM-5.2 passes vibe check; Z.ai forecasts Open Fable by December
blurb: Z.ai's GLM-5.2 is drawing consensus as the first open-weight model to credibly reach the frontier, with Artificial Analysis' knowledge-work benchmark scoring it above GPT-5.5 and practitioners on r/LocalLlama and elsewhere echoing the result. The validation marks Z.ai as a genuine frontier lab and sets up a December timeline for an open Fable-class model, narrowing the gap between open releases and the top four closed labs.

title: Is AI ruining our skills? Early results are in – and they're not good
blurb: Studies are finding measurable "deskilling" as professionals grow dependent on AI tools, with a Lancet study of expert endoscopists showing adenoma detection rates fell from 28.4% to 22.4% on AI-free colonoscopies after the tool was introduced. Surveys echo the concern, with 70% of nurses and 77% of physicians worried about losing skills, prompting researchers to debate which human expertise is worth preserving versus outsourcing.

title: The departure of John Jumper, a key member of Google's AI coding development team, further strains Google's efforts to compete with Anthropic and OpenAI (Bloomberg)
blurb: John Jumper, a key member of Google's AI coding development team, has departed, dealing another blow to the company's bid to keep pace with Anthropic and OpenAI. His exit deepens a talent drain at a moment when coding agents have become a central battleground, and Google is racing to close the gap on rivals whose models lead on developer-facing benchmarks.

title: Kaspersky finds malware hidden in Steam Wallpaper Engine that hijacks accounts to spread itself — dozens of malicious packages downloaded tens of thousands of times
blurb: Kaspersky found dozens of malware-laced Wallpaper Engine packages on Steam that hijack accounts to upload more infected wallpapers, mostly targeting gamers in China.

title: Unpatchable 'usbliter8' Exploit Breaks Apple A12 and A13 SecureROM Boot Chain
blurb: Researchers at Paradigm Shift published usbliter8, a working exploit that achieves arbitrary code execution inside the SecureROM of Apple's A12 and A13 chips by abusing a flaw in the Synopsys DWC2 USB controller. Because the code is burned into silicon, no software update can fix it, though the attack needs physical access to a device in DFU mode and finishes in under two seconds.

title: AI data centers just got a government-mandated fast lane to the grid
blurb: FERC ordered six major grid operators to fast-track interconnection requests from data centers and other large power users, with the centers paying their own connection costs. The unanimous orders also push operators to consider alternative transmission tech like solid-state transformers, but they sidestep the underlying generating-capacity shortage as data center electricity demand is projected to nearly triple through 2035.

title: Tensordyne makes a big bet on log math to beat Nvidia
blurb: Tensordyne is betting on logarithmic number systems to undercut Nvidia, replacing the compute-hungry multiplications in AI workloads with cheaper additions of logarithms. The startup claims the approach delivers competitive performance per watt for inference, though it still has to prove the math holds up against entrenched GPU tooling and Nvidia's software ecosystem.

title: The $13 Billion AI Startup Betting on Cheaper Alternatives to OpenAI, Anthropic
blurb: Baseten is raising $1.5B in a new round that would value the AI infrastructure startup at $13B, betting on demand for cheaper alternatives to OpenAI and Anthropic. The company sells services that help customers run lower-cost open and custom AI models, part of a growing Silicon Valley ecosystem competing on inference price rather than frontier model quality.

title: Adobe embeds agentic AI workflows across Creative Cloud, shifting from media generation to production orchestration
blurb: Adobe launched a public beta of its Creative Agent across Premiere Pro, Photoshop, Illustrator, InDesign, and Frame.io, shifting its AI from generating flat media to orchestrating multi-step production workflows. The embedded assistant taps each app's existing APIs to handle tedious tasks like sorting source media, batch-renaming clips, and updating brand assets across layouts, while leaving final aesthetic decisions to the human designer.

title: Mastodon 4.6 adds profile Collections and two-factor controls
blurb: Mastodon 4.6 adds Collections for sharing curated profile lists and gives server admins a switch to require two-factor authentication.

title: Microsoft launches Copilot Cowork globally for Microsoft 365
blurb: Microsoft made Copilot Cowork generally available worldwide for Microsoft 365 Copilot customers, following a three-month preview adopted by over half the Fortune 500. The cloud-hosted agentic system runs long, multi-tool tasks end-to-end to deliver finished outputs, billed per usage in Copilot Credits, with model choice spanning Anthropic Opus 4.8, Sonnet 4.6, GPT-5.5, and an upcoming lower-cost Cowork 1.

title: Databricks expands Lakehouse to unify OLAP and OLTP
blurb: Databricks introduced LTAP, a Lakehouse architecture that unifies analytical (OLAP) and transactional (OLTP) processing in a single storage layer governed by Unity Catalog. Announced at its Data + AI Summit, LTAP builds on Lakebase, the company's serverless Postgres layer that already handles 12 million database launches per day, letting operational data be queried for analytics with no pipelines or copying between formats.

title: From PGP to Mythos: a brief history of export controls that didn’t stop anyone
blurb: The author argues that the White House's order forcing Anthropic to restrict exports of its Fable and Mythos models echoes decades of failed attempts to contain cyber technology through export controls, from 1990s encryption to commercial spyware. History suggests the rules rarely stop proliferation and instead reshape which markets labs can reach, making this standoff a template every frontier AI lab will have to build around.

title: The Korean telecom giant at the center of Anthropic's Mythos controversy
blurb: The Trump administration ordered Anthropic to revoke access to its most powerful models, Claude Mythos and Fable 5, for all foreign nationals after officials grew alarmed that SK Telecom, a recipient they allege has ties to China, gained access through the company's Project Glasswing program. Rather than gate access by nationality, Anthropic disabled the models entirely, and the two sides remain deadlocked after days of negotiations.

title: Show HN: Are You in the Weights?
blurb: A web tool that checks whether your name or website appears in the training data of major LLMs.

title: Microsoft new Outlook takes 10 seconds to do what Outlook Classic does instantly
blurb: Microsoft's new Outlook for Windows takes roughly 10 seconds to open an email from a notification banner, a task Outlook Classic completes almost instantly. The WebView2-based app is essentially a browser window loading Outlook.com, and the performance gap helps explain why Microsoft pushed its forced-migration deadline for enterprises back a full year to March 2027.

title: TerraPower in deal with Meta for eight Natrium 345 MW nuclear plants
blurb: TerraPower and Meta announced an agreement to develop up to eight 345 MW Natrium nuclear reactors, with Meta funding early work on the first two units and rights to power from six more. It is Meta's first direct investment in a new nuclear build, with initial units expected as early as 2032 at an estimated $2.1B per reactor to feed the company's AI data centers.

title: [AINews] Midjourney Medical: scan your organs like you step on a scale
blurb: Midjourney announced the Midjourney Scanner, a full-body ultrasonic CT system that founder David Holz called the first new whole-body imaging modality in 50 years. The Gen 1 prototype uses 358,000 ultrasonic elements across a 70cm ring to capture body slices at 0.5mm resolution without radiation, marking the bootstrapped image-generation lab's first move into medical hardware.

title: Companies rein in AI usage as costs strain budgets
blurb: Companies are scaling back AI deployments as inference and licensing costs outpace the budgets set during last year's adoption rush, according to the FT. The shift suggests the spending bottleneck has moved from proving AI works to making it pay off, with finance teams now scrutinizing per-query costs that engineering enthusiasm initially obscured.

title: 2,000 retired Google Pixel phones get a second life as a private cloud
blurb: Engineers turned 2,000 retired Google Pixel phones into a working private cloud, repurposing the handsets as compute nodes rather than scrapping them as e-waste. The build shows how decommissioned consumer hardware, with its capable ARM processors and built-in batteries, can be reracked into a low-cost server fleet that doubles as a case study in extending device lifespans.

title: NASA asks Northrop Grumman to stop working on lunar HALO module
blurb: NASA told Northrop Grumman to halt work on HALO, the $1.1B pressurized habitation module built for the now-paused Lunar Gateway. The directive extends to subcontractor Paragon Space Development, which held a $100M-plus contract for the module's life-support system, signaling that NASA's pivot from a lunar-orbit station to a Moon surface base leaves HALO without a mission.

title: How Hackers Found a Back Door Into the American Living Room
blurb: Nation-state hackers are routing attacks through residential proxy networks, hijacking everyday consumer electronics like smart TVs and routers to disguise malicious traffic as ordinary home internet activity. The technique makes attribution far harder for defenders, since the traffic appears to originate from millions of legitimate residential IP addresses rather than known hostile infrastructure.

title: Anthropic's Claude Code Artifacts update brings live, shared dashboards and interactive workspaces to enterprises
blurb: Anthropic added Artifacts to Claude Code for Team and Enterprise plans, turning a session's work into live, shareable HTML pages like dashboards and internal apps that update in real time at a fixed URL. The feature builds UIs directly from the user's repo and connected data sources, and lands two weeks after OpenAI shipped a similar enterprise hosting feature called Sites for Codex.

title: New AI optimization framework beats Claude Code and Codex by 2.5x on the same compute budget
blurb: Researchers at Renmin University of China and Microsoft Research introduced Arbor, an autonomous optimization framework that organizes hypotheses, experiments, and insights into a tree so an agent learns from prior failures instead of repeating trial-and-error. On real-world engineering tasks under the same compute budget, Arbor delivered more than 2.5x the verifiable performance gains of standard coding agents like Claude Code and Codex.

title: Microsoft confirms Windows 11 26H2, urges IT admins to prepare for release
blurb: Microsoft confirmed Windows 11 version 26H2 and is urging IT admins to begin preparing for its release, expected to roll out around early October 2026. The update ships as a small enablement package that toggles features already present in the code base rather than replacing the OS, making deployment quicker, though 26H1 users on the separate scoped branch cannot upgrade to it.

title: Snap Specs bring rich augmented reality to smart glasses
blurb: Snap released the Specs, augmented-reality glasses that pair a headset-grade immersive display with the lightweight build of AI glasses, shown this week at the Augmented World Expo in Long Beach. The design targets entertainment, mobile work, and on-the-go assistance, signaling Snap's push to fold full mixed-reality into a form factor people will actually wear all day.

title: The enormous size of the oil and gas market drives adoption of synthetic fuel production
blurb: The author argues that synthetic fuels can reach mass adoption because the oil and gas market is large enough to support both a profitable beachhead and the volume needed for economies of scale to drive costs down. He frames Terraform's path through Wright's Law and Tesla's Roadster-to-Model-Y playbook, where early premium buyers fund the cost declines that eventually open the broader market.

title: Apple announces major App Store changes for Brazil, including alternative app marketplaces
blurb: Apple opened iOS in Brazil to alternative app marketplaces, settling a CADE antitrust dispute that MercadoLibre triggered in 2022. Developers using the MarketplaceKit framework can now distribute outside the App Store and offer non-Apple payment methods, though web sideloading stays blocked and a new commission structure mirrors the rules Apple rolled out in Japan.

title: Adobe expands Firefly capabilities, extends agentic tools to Creative Cloud apps
blurb: Adobe expanded its Firefly AI assistant with pre-built Creative Skills, including a brand kit generator that produces logos, color palettes, and social media videos from a single prompt. The company also added agentic Firefly sidebars to Premiere, Photoshop, Illustrator, InDesign, and Frame.io, letting users delegate tasks like renaming assets, fact-checking against briefs, and applying brand guideline changes.

title: Operation Endgame Disrupts SocGholish Servers, Cleans 14,971 WordPress Sites
blurb: Operation Endgame disrupted SocGholish, taking down 106 servers and cleaning 14,971 infected WordPress sites across four countries.

title: Exclusive: Microsoft evaluates different open models for Cowork
blurb: Microsoft is evaluating a range of open and open-weight models beyond DeepSeek to power Copilot Cowork, its enterprise agentic work app, according to TestingCatalog. The architecture separates a stable orchestration harness from a swappable model layer, letting Microsoft route tasks to frontier APIs or cheaper self-hosted Azure models by cost and capability while reducing dependence on any single provider.

title: Mistral AI to get Code and Apps features on Vibe
blurb: Mistral AI is building Code and Apps sections into the web version of Le Chat (Vibe), adding a browser-based coding surface alongside an artifacts-style area for building, hosting, and sharing apps via connectors and workflows. The features land as CEO Arthur Mensch confirmed a new open-weights model this summer, a large sparse mixture-of-experts design with partner access starting in July, signaling Mistral's push from model lab to full product platform.

title: Trump says he saw Anthropic last week as a national security threat, but signals relations have since improved because Dario Amodei was "nice" and "smart" at G7 (Maria Curi/Axios)
blurb: Trump said he viewed Anthropic as a national security threat last week but indicated relations have improved after meeting CEO Dario Amodei at the G7, where he described Amodei as "nice" and "smart." The softened stance follows tension over Anthropic's safety-focused public positioning, and signals the administration may be recalibrating its posture toward the frontier lab as AI policy fights intensify.

title: From $5B to $13B in five months: Baseten reportedly closing in on $1.5B raise
blurb: Baseten is reportedly nearing a $1.5B funding round at up to a $13B valuation, just five months after its $300M Series E valued the AI inference platform at $5B. Prior backers Conviction and Spark Capital are co-leading the split-priced deal, reflecting investor attention shifting from training frontier models to the infrastructure needed to run them in production.

title: Hey, n00b, we didn't hire you to complete tasks
blurb: Kent Beck argues that junior engineers aren't hired to complete tasks, since a tech lead could finish the same work faster, but as an "option premium" on the senior they may become. He frames a junior's real job as sorting themselves into the top tier by signaling growth, because seniors triage their limited mentoring time toward the people most likely to make it.

title: Perplexity releases Brain Memory System for Perplexity Computer
blurb: Perplexity released Brain, a shared memory system that sits beneath products like Perplexity Search and its Computer agent rather than living inside any one of them. Unlike most opaque assistant memory, Brain exposes the full knowledge base to users as categorized topics, the context behind each, and a navigable 3D map of their connections, and it pulls context only when a task needs it.

title: I hate compilers
blurb: Anubis author Xe Iaso compiled the `wasm2js` tool to WebAssembly and vendored it into the bot-protection system, so its WebAssembly proof-of-work check and its JavaScript fallback produce bit-for-bit identical logic from a single source. Reaching reproducible builds meant fighting three compiler quirks: timestamp macros breaking determinism, Clang silently shelling out to a system `wasm-opt`, and address-sensitive codegen that varied output by ~29 bytes across architectures.

title: Emacs 31 is around the corner: The changes I'm daily driving
blurb: Emacs 31 brings automatic tree-sitter grammar installation and built-in mode grammar sources, eliminating manual treesit-language-source-alist setup.

title: I told them forced consent was unlawful. 5 years later it cost Elkjop €1.8M
blurb: A privacy advocate recounts how a 2021 complaint that Elkjop's Nordic customer club forced members to cancel their membership just to opt out of marketing emails ended in a €1.8M GDPR fine five years later. The post walks through the legal mechanics, from Article 21(2) objection rights and freely-given consent under Article 7 to how a Swedish complaint to IMY became a Norwegian enforcement action.

title: Many Let's Encrypt renewals had errors today
blurb: Let's Encrypt reported errors affecting many certificate renewals today, though its ACME API and status components now show operational.

title: SteamOS Linux 3.8 released as stable
blurb: Valve released SteamOS 3.8 as a stable build, the Arch Linux-based operating system that powers the Steam Deck and its gaming-focused desktop and handheld mode. The release continues Valve's push to extend SteamOS beyond its own hardware to third-party handhelds, strengthening Linux as a viable PC gaming platform against Windows.

title: ChatGPT's image generator can be manipulated to produce violent, sexual content
blurb: Mindgard research found that ChatGPT's image generator can be manipulated into producing violent and sexually explicit content, including depictions of death and sexual violence, without users directly requesting it. The findings follow earlier reports of the tool generating nude images that OpenAI said it had fixed, and renew questions about why these models are trained on such material and why content filters keep failing.

title: Canonical reveals Myna, its local speech-to-text app
blurb: Canonical introduced Myna, a local speech-to-text app for Ubuntu that runs AI transcription on-device rather than in the cloud.

title: Microsoft will soon allow some users to block Copilot from analyzing their Office files
blurb: Microsoft is updating a Purview sensitivity setting to let commercial customers block Copilot and other connected AI tools from analyzing labeled Word, Excel, and PowerPoint files. The change, tagged MC1297982, enforces blocking across all connected services and applies automatically to already-labeled files, with phased general availability completing by the end of next month for customers on qualifying Purview licenses.

title: OpenAI prepares GPT-5.6 models for the upcoming release
blurb: OpenAI is preparing to release its GPT-5.6 model family as soon as Tuesday, likely spanning standard, Mini, and Pro variants alongside a bidirectional voice model codenamed GPT-Bidi-1. Reports point to a context window near 1.5M tokens, stronger agentic coding, and deeper price cuts that would widen OpenAI's roughly 2x token-cost lead over Anthropic, whose Mythos-tier Claude Fable 5 faces US regulatory uncertainty.

title: GLM-5.2 Becomes the Top Open-Weight Model on Artificial Analysis
blurb: Artificial Analysis named Z.ai's GLM-5.2 the top open-weight model on its Intelligence Index, scoring 51 under an MIT license.

title: .gitignore Isn't the only way to ignore files in Git
blurb: Git supports three levels of file ignoring beyond the familiar checked-in .gitignore. Per-repo rules can live in .git/info/exclude without being committed, and machine-wide rules go in ~/.config/git/ignore, which is ideal for noise like .DS_Store across every repository. Use git check-ignore -v to see which file and line is actually ignoring a given path.

title: Google told researcher 'Nice catch!' Then denied bug bounty for flaw it still hasn't fixed
blurb: A security researcher named Justin O'Leary reported a Config Connector flaw, dubbed ConfigConfusion, that lets any Kubernetes namespace user bypass GCP's IAM controls and gain owner-level access to an entire Google Cloud organization. Google initially accepted the report as P1/S1 and praised the catch, then reversed course, declaring there was no vulnerability, paying no bounty, and leaving the bug unfixed since the March 8 disclosure.

title: KDE Plasma 6.7 brings the X11 era to a close
blurb: KDE Plasma 6.7 ships with X11 session support effectively deprecated, marking the desktop environment's full pivot to Wayland as the default and primary display server. The release continues KDE's multi-year migration away from the decades-old X Window System, joining GNOME and other Linux desktops in treating X11 as legacy rather than a maintained path forward.

title: Hyundai buys Boston Dynamics
blurb: Hyundai will acquire SoftBank's remaining 9.65% stake in Boston Dynamics for $325 million, expected to close June 22 and making the Waltham robotics company a wholly owned subsidiary. The deal completes Hyundai's takeover that began with an $880 million majority purchase in 2021, and positions its factories as the first deployment site for the electric Atlas humanoid, which is slated to start work at a Georgia EV plant by 2028.

title: A new bill takes aim at government pressure to silence lawful online speech
blurb: Senators Ted Cruz and Ron Wyden introduced the bipartisan JAWBONE Act, which would create a federal cause of action against government officials who coerce broadcasters, online platforms, or AI providers into suppressing lawful, First-Amendment-protected speech. The bill also sets up a transparency system for government communications with intermediaries about user expression, giving censored parties a legal right to sue beyond what the First Amendment already provides.

title: NASA’s new rover prototype drove 16 miles in a week, 10 times faster than anything it has on Mars
blurb: NASA's JPL drove its ERNEST rover prototype 16 miles in 37 hours during California desert testing, hitting 0.6mph, roughly 10 times faster than Curiosity or Perseverance manage on Mars. The four-wheeled machine pairs mesh wheels and an active suspension with two powered joints per wheel against navigation software trained via reinforcement learning, letting it climb slopes and obstacles that strand current rovers.

title: The Token Compression Illusion: Why I'm Skeptical of RTK
blurb: An engineer argues RTK's headline "60-90% token savings" only reflects stripped Bash output, ignores the real cost drivers, and risks silently mangling agent context.

title: New usage analytics and updated spend controls for enterprises
blurb: ChatGPT Enterprise adds usage analytics and updated spend controls to help organizations track and cap AI costs.

title: AI nose uses 'Smell Language Model' to sniff out signs of disease
blurb: Researchers built an AI "nose" that pairs a chemical sensor array with a Smell Language Model, a system that interprets odor signatures the way large language models parse text. Trained to recognize the volatile compounds linked to conditions like cancer and diabetes, the device aims to flag disease from breath or body odor, offering a noninvasive screening path that still needs clinical validation.

title: Why Apple’s War Chest Can’t Win the Memory War
blurb: Apple’s purchasing power, long a lever for locking in cheap components, has weakened as the AI boom drives surging demand and prices for memory chips. With cloud and AI firms outbidding for the same DRAM and flash, Apple’s scale no longer guarantees favorable supply terms, squeezing margins on devices that depend on ever-larger memory configurations.

title: Sources: PC makers, including HP, are in talks with their supply-chain partners about using CXMT's memory chips in products bound for Asia as DRAM prices soar (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: PC makers including HP are in talks with supply-chain partners about using memory chips from China's CXMT in products bound for Asia, according to the Wall Street Journal. The move would help manufacturers sidestep surging DRAM prices, marking a notable opening for CXMT as it pushes to compete with Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron.

title: OpenAI gets the attention it needs from AI researcher Noam Shazeer
blurb: Noam Shazeer, co-author of "Attention Is All You Need" and a creator of the transformer architecture behind modern AI models, is leaving Google for OpenAI. Shazeer had returned to Google when it acquired his startup Character.AI for $2.7B and most recently co-led the Gemini project, making his departure a notable talent win for OpenAI ahead of a possible IPO.

title: Trump Tells Axios He Doesn’t See Anthropic as US Security Threat
blurb: President Trump told Axios he does not consider Anthropic a national-security threat, walking back the tension days after his administration moved to block foreign access to the company's most advanced AI models. The comments leave the export restrictions in place while signaling that the White House sees Anthropic as a domestic asset rather than a risk, easing pressure on a key US frontier lab.

title: Your old iPhone may have a security flaw Apple can’t fix
blurb: Security researchers at Paradigm Shift detailed usbliter8, a BootROM exploit affecting iPhones and Apple Watches with A12, A13, S4, and S5 chips, including the iPhone XR, XS, and 11 lines. Because the flaw sits in chip-level startup code Apple cannot patch on shipped devices, the only mitigations are avoiding untrusted USB connections or upgrading, though the attack requires physical USB access rather than remote delivery.

title: The 11 standout startups from YC’s Demo Day, according to VCs
blurb: Y Combinator's Spring 2026 Demo Day featured a cohort heavy on defense tech, robotics, AI infrastructure, and agents, with TechCrunch surveying eight VCs to flag the 11 buzziest startups. Counter-drone startup 9 Mothers led the batch at a valuation north of $200 million, part of a season where at least two companies cleared $175 million as investors paid premiums for repeat founders.

title: He made your free video player run smoothly. Now he’s doing that for robots.
blurb: Kyber, a Paris-based startup from VLC lead developer Jean-Baptiste Kempf, raised a $5M round led by Lightspeed to build infrastructure for controlling remote robots and drones in real time. Its core SDK synchronizes video, audio, sensor data, and control inputs with minimal latency, targeting fleets that could scale to millions of devices as physical AI shifts the bottleneck to the systems running underneath it.

title: Android verification is coming: Google confirms timeline and supported app stores
blurb: Google confirmed its Android developer verification system will begin rolling out September 30, starting in Brazil, Indonesia, Singapore, and Thailand, with unverified developers' apps blocked from sideloading on certified devices. The $25 verification requirement extends to trusted third-party stores, so a developer verified in one participating storefront is verified across Google's system, easing a change that critics say erodes Android's open distribution model.

title: Every fusion startup that has raised over $100M
blurb: Private fusion startups have now collectively raised billions, with Commonwealth Fusion Systems alone holding nearly $3 billion after an $863M round that closed in August. TechCrunch's roundup of every fusion company past the $100M mark reflects investor confidence built on high-temperature superconducting magnets, better AI-driven simulations, and the 2022 scientific breakeven milestone at a US Department of Energy lab.

title: Is the US government’s Anthropic ban accidentally helping the brand?
blurb: The US government forced Anthropic to pull its two newest models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, citing national security concerns after Amazon researchers reportedly found a way to bypass Fable 5's guardrails. Cybersecurity researchers signed an open letter calling the ban dangerous, and Anthropic noted the same jailbreaks exist in rival models, leaving developers building on the platform uncertain ahead of a closely watched IPO.

title: Boeing Completes Quantum Lab Test Ahead of 2027 Flight
blurb: Boeing demonstrated high-fidelity quantum entanglement swapping in ground tests of its Q4S quantum networking satellite, clearing the path for a 2027 launch. The one-year orbital demonstration will generate four entangled photons to create a network effect rather than a point-to-point link, aiming to prove that quantum repeaters can work in space and lay groundwork for global-scale secure communications.

title: Boeing demonstrates quantum protocol in payload set for 2027 launch
blurb: Boeing demonstrated high-fidelity entanglement swapping in ground tests of its compact Q4S quantum networking payload, ahead of a one-year on-orbit experiment slated for 2027. The protocol, which extends links between entangled photon pairs through teleportation, is a core building block of quantum networks, and proving it works within a spacecraft's size, weight, and power limits is a step toward a space-based quantum internet.

title: Norway greenlights first full-scale ship tunnel
blurb: Norway approved NOK 8.6bn (£671m) to build the Stad Ship Tunnel, the world's first full-scale ship tunnel, with construction starting in early 2027. The nearly 2km passage lets coastal ferries bypass the Stad peninsula, one of Norway's most dangerous stretches of coast where storms strike roughly 100 days a year, and is expected to open to traffic in 2031.

title: Bosch to pay $36 million penalty for $72 million in ‘illicit’ sales to Huawei — German company sold export-controlled goods and software to banned Chinese firm between 2020 and 2024
blurb: Bosch agreed to pay a $36 million penalty for selling export-controlled MEMS sensors and automotive software to Huawei between 2020 and 2024, in violation of US export controls covering more than $72 million in sales. The Justice Department declined to prosecute after Bosch self-disclosed and cooperated, but the case signals tougher enforcement of rules that also bind US-origin technology sold through non-US subsidiaries.

title: Frontier Airlines site leaks all personal info with just a glance at a boarding pass, researcher claims — booking number and last name nets you every passenger's personal info, including address, passport, TSA PreCheck, and most credit card info
blurb: A security researcher found that Frontier Airlines' API and website exposed every passenger's personal data to anyone holding a boarding pass, requiring only the six-digit booking code and a last name to return home addresses, full passport details, partial credit card numbers, and TSA PreCheck codes. The researcher reported the flaws over three months ago and published them after Frontier made only minimal fixes, leaving data usable for identity theft and security bypasses.

title: Swiss parliament lifts ban on new nuclear power plants
blurb: Switzerland's National Council voted 100 to 98 to lift the country's ban on building new nuclear power plants, aligning with the Federal Council and Council of States after a marathon debate. The reversal of the 2017 ban, passed as a counterproposal to the Blackout Initiative, still faces a final public referendum before construction can resume.

title: The US says ASML’s top chip tool may be in China. ASML says it isn’t
blurb: US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick has privately told ASML executives he suspects one of the company's EUV lithography machines, the only tools capable of printing the most advanced chips, may have reached China in breach of export controls. ASML denies any such system exists there, and administration officials have declined to share their evidence with either Bloomberg or the company.

title: Telegram ban in India sparks a rush to VPNs, rival apps
blurb: India temporarily blocked Telegram for a week over exam-fraud concerns, triggering the country's largest VPN download surge since at least early 2025 as users routed around the restriction. VPN downloads jumped 49% to 208,000 in a day, with Proton VPN climbing to second in Google Play's Tools category and Proton reporting Indian registrations up 120% above baseline.

title: A bold satellite rescue mission came together in record time, but will it work?
blurb: NASA awarded Katalyst Space Technologies a $30M contract to build and launch a satellite that will chase down the aging Swift observatory and boost it back to a safe orbit. Swift, a $500M gamma-ray burst mission with no thrusters of its own, has decayed from 363 to 225 miles since 2004, and Katalyst plans to latch on with three robotic arms in under a year.

title: NASA selects Eric Schmidt’s rocket company for a 2028 mission to Mars
blurb: NASA selected Relativity Space, the rocket company now led by former Google CEO Eric Schmidt, to launch its Aeolus payload to Mars in 2028 under a new public-private partnership. Relativity will supply the spacecraft, rocket, and cruise operations to deliver four instruments that will produce the first daily global view of Martian winds, temperatures, dust, and clouds to inform future landing systems.

title: A startup betting on a different material for AI’s optics problem just raised $80mn from the people who build the hardware
blurb: HyperLight, a Harvard spinout building optical interconnects on thin-film lithium niobate instead of silicon, raised $80M in a Series C led by MediaTek. The round drew in Foxconn, Jabil, UMC, and the Qatar Investment Authority, an AI-hardware supply chain syndicate betting that TFLN can move data between GPU clusters at higher speeds and lower power than copper as clusters scale to hundreds of thousands of chips.

title: Paris-based Kyber, which develops a low-latency remote device control SDK and is founded by VLC lead developer Jean-Baptiste Kempf, raised $5M led by Lightspeed (Anna Heim/TechCrunch)
blurb: Kyber, a Paris-based startup building a low-latency SDK for remote device control, raised $5M in a round led by Lightspeed. The company was founded by Jean-Baptiste Kempf, lead developer of VLC, the open-source media player installed on billions of devices.

title: Amazon investigating engineers who criticized AI data center expansion
blurb: Amazon is investigating three engineers who testified before the Seattle City Council against the company's rapid AI data center expansion and called for stronger regulation. The employees, who say they were warned that discipline could extend to termination, filed a complaint alleging Amazon violated a city ordinance barring political-ideology discrimination, while Amazon says it is checking whether they improperly spoke as company representatives.

title: Accenture Takes a Hit on Worsening Outlook and Cloudy AI Future
blurb: Accenture shares fell 18% after the IT consulting firm cut its revenue outlook, with the CEO warning of weaker bookings in the months ahead. The drop reflects mounting investor concern that generative AI is eroding demand for the traditional consulting and systems-integration work that has long anchored Accenture's business.

title: Elon Musk’s Next Move May Be a Mega-Merger of SpaceX and Tesla
blurb: Elon Musk is reportedly exploring a merger of SpaceX and Tesla, which would combine his rocket and electric-vehicle companies into a single entity. Legal experts say that while some shareholders could object to the deal, they would have little power to block it given Musk's control over both companies.

title: Game-clip AI startup General Intuition in talks to raise $300M at $2B valuation
blurb: General Intuition, a New York startup that trains embodied AI agents on first-person video game footage, is in talks to raise about $300M at a valuation just over $2B. The round would roughly quadruple the $134M seed valuation it set eight months ago, drawing on Medal's dataset of 2 billion gameplay clips a year to train world models that teach agents to navigate physical space.

title: Chinese startup Spark Space tests engine, raises funds for electric-pump rocket
blurb: Chinese startup Spark Space raised two Pre-A rounds totaling over $14.8 million to build the Jinhua-1, which it claims will be the world's largest electric-pump-fed rocket. The two-stage vehicle targets a 2027 debut with 1,500 kg to low Earth orbit, roughly five times Rocket Lab's Electron, following the first hot-fire tests of its Lieyan-2 engine in March.

title: AltStore PAL now available in Brazil as Apple flips the switch on alternative marketplaces [U]
blurb: Apple enabled alternative app marketplaces in Brazil, letting users install AltStore PAL and set it as their default storefront under iOS Settings. The change follows a settlement with competition watchdog CADE that also opened iOS to alternative payment systems, making Brazil the third region after Japan and the EU to allow third-party app distribution.

title: Ditching the cloud for local AI — how I use two mini PCs to process millions of tokens a day and save money on costly API fees
blurb: A Tom's Hardware writer details running local AI on two mini PCs after buying a £1,500 ($2,000) GMKtec machine with a Ryzen AI Max+ 395 and 96GB of RAM to avoid recurring API and subscription costs. The author argues that improving open-weight models, cheaper capable hardware, and tools like Ollama and LM Studio make a one-off hardware purchase plus electricity cheaper than escalating cloud inference bills for heavy users.

title: NVIDIA joins $54M bet on startup that gets AI data centres online three years faster
blurb: Verse raised $54M in a Series B led by Bessemer Venture Partners, with NVIDIA also investing, to deploy on-site battery storage that lets AI data centers skip multi-year grid interconnection queues. The approach can bring sites online up to three years sooner without cutting compute, addressing waits that now run five to seven years at major hubs.

title: Hospitals and universities repurposing drugs at lower cost
blurb: Universities and hospitals are running late-stage clinical trials to repurpose generic drugs at up to 90% lower cost than pharmaceutical companies, according to research from King's College London published in the Cambridge Law Journal. Operating outside the patent system, this parallel pipeline has already redirected existing drugs to new uses, including a cancer drug for a leading cause of blindness and an old anti-inflammatory for Covid.

title: France’s OVHcloud bets on frontier AI as Europe seeks alternatives to US models
blurb: OVHcloud, one of Europe's largest homegrown cloud providers, plans to train a family of frontier AI models from scratch and open-source them once they hit performance targets, CEO Octave Klaba told Reuters. The move pits OVHcloud against Paris-based Mistral as Europe seeks alternatives to US and Chinese systems, with Klaba pegging training costs below $230M, down from roughly $1.15B, though analysts note serving and updates carry the larger bill.

title: Snap spins off AI video team into new company, Dotmo, due to costs
blurb: Snap is spinning off its internal generative AI video team into a separate company, Dotmo, citing the high cost of running the work in-house. The new venture will build AI models for interactive gaming, staffed by departing Snap employees and led as lead investor by CTO Bobby Murphy, while Snap takes a large equity stake in exchange for talent and a technology license.

title: As China looms, Taiwan makes more drones for defense and the US military
blurb: Taiwan's defense ministry proposed a $6.6B special budget to buy more than 208,000 domestically made coastal attack drones, 1,400 reconnaissance drones, and 1,320 uncrewed surface vessels between 2026 and 2031. The buildout doubles as an export play, with Taiwanese firms like Thunder Tiger pitching China-free drones to the US military and European buyers after shipping $115M abroad in the first quarter alone.

title: Indian Software Stocks Tumble After Accenture Warns on Growth
blurb: Indian software stocks tumbled Friday after Accenture forecast slower revenue growth, with Tata Consultancy Services falling as much as 6.5% to its lowest since 2020 and Infosys sliding 8.7%. The selloff deepens a rough year for the sector, which has shed nearly 30% of its market value as concerns mount over AI eroding demand for outsourced IT services.

title: AMD will reinstate memory encryption on Ryzen 9000 CPUs through a BIOS update in July — TSME is coming back after 'valuable community feedback'
blurb: AMD will reinstate Transparent Secure Memory Encryption (TSME) on consumer Ryzen 9000 desktop CPUs via a BIOS update in July, reversing a quiet removal made earlier this year in the AGESA 1.2.7.0 firmware. The feature encrypts data in RAM to guard against cold boot attacks, and AMD said the reversal came "based on valuable community feedback" after a security researcher flagged the change on GitHub.

title: Apple CEO Tim Cook warns AI-driven price increases are unavoidable — says company is trying its best but 'the situation has become unsustainable'
blurb: Apple CEO Tim Cook told the Wall Street Journal that price increases on Apple products are now unavoidable, citing surging costs for LPDDR and 3D NAND memory the company can no longer fully absorb. Cook declined to specify timing or magnitude, but TechInsights estimates a flagship iPhone Pro would need roughly a $270 hike to hold current margins, a problem compounded by Apple's growing memory demands from on-device AI.

title: Midjourney pivots from AI image generation to body scanning medical spa where patients bathe in 'golden light'
blurb: Midjourney is launching a body-scanning medical spa where patients bathe in "golden light," pivoting away from its core AI image generation business. The Register reports the underlying scanning technology is real but borrowed from an unnamed partner the company never disclosed, raising questions about how much of the venture Midjourney actually built itself.

title: Sources: Abu Dhabi's MGX is exploring buying Singapore-based data center operator DayOne; last month, sources said DayOne planned a US IPO at a $20B valuation (Reuters)
blurb: Abu Dhabi's MGX is exploring an acquisition of DayOne, the Singapore-based data center operator, according to Reuters sources. The talks come a month after reports that DayOne was preparing a US IPO at a $20B valuation, suggesting the AI-fueled rush for data center capacity is pulling the company toward a sale instead.

title: Q&A with Signal's Meredith Whittaker on why online child safety efforts risk mass surveillance, leaving the markets that demand weakening of encryption, more (Mishal Husain/Bloomberg)
blurb: In a Bloomberg Q&A, Signal president Meredith Whittaker argues that online child-safety mandates push platforms toward client-side scanning that amounts to mass surveillance and breaks end-to-end encryption. She says Signal would rather exit markets that legally require weakening its encryption than comply, framing the child-safety debate as a backdoor route to undermining private communication for everyone.

title: South Korean media: Hyundai plans to buy SoftBank's remaining 9.65% stake in Boston Dynamics for $325M to make the US robotics company a wholly owned subsidiary (Reuters)
blurb: Hyundai plans to buy SoftBank's remaining 9.65% stake in Boston Dynamics for $325M, making the US robotics company a wholly owned subsidiary, according to South Korean media. The deal would give Hyundai full control as it pushes Boston Dynamics' Atlas humanoid and Spot robots toward commercial deployment, valuing the firm at roughly $3.4B.

title: Secretive Wall Street Powerhouse Jane Street Seizes the AI Spotlight
blurb: Jane Street, the secretive quantitative trading firm, has emerged as a major force in AI by applying its machine-learning expertise to markets and aggressively recruiting top researchers. The firm has grown from a handful of staffers to 3,500 and plans to hire more than 500 employees this year, putting it in direct competition with frontier labs for talent.

title: Blue Origin begins rebuilding New Glenn pad
blurb: Blue Origin has begun rebuilding the Cape Canaveral launch pad destroyed in a May 28 New Glenn explosion during a static-fire test, aiming to resume flights before the end of the year. The blast collapsed a lightning tower and destroyed the transporter-erector, but the propellant tank farm and a reusable booster survived, and the company plans an alternative vertical setup that skips replacing the transporter-erector.

title: Next year to be Apple’s ‘biggest product year’ ever, here’s what’s coming
blurb: Bloomberg's Mark Gurman said 2027 will be Apple's biggest product year ever, with a roadmap that reportedly includes six new iPhones, two AI wearables, and a wave of M6-based Macs and iPads. The lineup centers on 20th-anniversary iPhone 20 Pro models with near bezel-free quad-curved displays, alongside a new robotic tabletop home hub and updated Apple Watch models.

title: Why the Memory Crunch Is Almost Impossible to Solve
blurb: Memory chips have become the tech industry's tightest bottleneck as AI demand outstrips production capacity and supply stays effectively fixed for years. The author argues the crunch is structurally hard to fix because building new fabs takes years, the market is concentrated among a few suppliers, and national-security limits on Chinese chips remove a relief valve, leaving consumer devices to absorb rising prices.

title: Google Is Using Nvidia’s Playbook to Build a Rival AI Chip Business
blurb: Google is leveraging its data-center scale and cash reserves to sell its in-house TPU chips to outside customers, directly challenging Nvidia's dominance in AI silicon. The company is borrowing Nvidia's own strategy of bundling chips with software and infrastructure to court data-center clients, positioning its tensor processing units as an alternative to the GPUs that power most AI training today.

title: Midjourney, the AI Image Generator Company, is Making an MRI Scanner
blurb: Midjourney launched Midjourney Medical, a hardware division building a scanner that the company claims rivals MRI imaging using ultrasonic sensors, completing a full-body scan in 60 seconds for a fraction of an MRI's cost. The pivot comes as Midjourney has lost ground in AI image generation to Google and OpenAI, and marks an unexpected jump from software into capital-intensive medical hardware.

title: Apple opens up third-party app stores in Brazil
blurb: Apple is enabling third-party app stores for iOS users in Brazil, with a 5% Core Technology Fee for apps distributed outside the App Store.

title: Apple clears the air on watchOS 27 skipping multiple models, and you can partly blame AI for it
blurb: Apple's watchOS 27 will skip five older devices, including the Series 6, 7, 8, SE 2, and the first-generation Apple Watch Ultra, which will receive only basic security updates. Apple says the new Siri AI and tap gesture features need the processing power of the S9 chip and later, effectively making silicon the cutoff for the update's marquee capabilities.

title: Nothing just scrapped its next affordable phone, and the problem is bigger than you think
blurb: Nothing's CMF sub-brand canceled its next budget phone this year, with co-founder Akis Evangelidis citing surging memory prices rather than any issue with the device itself. CEO Carl Pei said DRAM and NAND now exceed 50% of a smartphone's hardware cost, driven by AI data center demand, a squeeze that could push entry-level prices up or thin out RAM and storage tiers across the industry.

title: Apple CEO Tim Cook confirms looming price hikes due to memory shortages
blurb: Tim Cook confirmed in a Wall Street Journal interview that Apple will raise prices on some products, calling the increases "unavoidable" as memory and storage shortages make absorbing the costs "unsustainable." Cook blamed AI companies' heavy memory-chip allocation for the squeeze and said Apple will tap its cash reserves to boost supply but won't build its own factories, with iPads and Macs likely to see hikes first.

title: Announcing Amazon EC2 G7 instances accelerated by NVIDIA RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs
blurb: AWS made Amazon EC2 G7 instances generally available, becoming the first major cloud provider to offer NVIDIA RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs paired with custom sixth-generation Intel Xeon Scalable processors. The instances deliver up to 4.6x AI inference and 2.1x graphics performance over G6, with 32GB of memory per GPU, 700 Gbps of EFA networking, and up to 7.6TB of local NVMe storage.

title: Google Gemini co-lead Noam Shazeer leaves for OpenAI
blurb: Noam Shazeer, Google's VP of engineering and a co-lead of its Gemini models, announced he is leaving to join OpenAI, less than two years after returning to Google in its Character.AI partnership. The exit lands weeks after Google shipped Gemini 3.5 Flash and the Gemini Spark agent at I/O, and as OpenAI moves toward a confidentially filed IPO.

title: Midjourney Medical
blurb: Midjourney launched Midjourney Medical, a new health division built around a radiation-free full-body Ultrasonic CT scanner that uses 500,000 transducers to image the body in 60 seconds. The company plans to open its first scanning spa in San Francisco in 2027 and aims for a fleet of over 50,000 scanners handling a billion scans per month by 2031.

title: Zero-Touch OAuth for MCP
blurb: The Enterprise-Managed Authorization (EMA) extension for the Model Context Protocol is now stable, letting organizations manage MCP server access centrally through their existing identity provider. Instead of per-app OAuth, the client obtains an Identity Assertion JWT Authorization Grant during single sign-on and exchanges it for an access token, giving users zero-touch setup and security teams central policy and audit control. Anthropic, Microsoft, and Okta are among early adopters.

title: Embedding Forbidden Text in Spyware to Discourage AI Analysis
blurb: A malware developer is embedding fake system instructions and weapons-related "forbidden" text inside JavaScript block comments in their spyware to derail AI-based code analysis. The header is inert to Node, Bun, and Python runtimes but targets scanners and analyst copilots that feed file contents to a language model without isolating them as untrusted data, potentially triggering refusals, context pollution, or premature classification before the real payload is reached. Schneier notes this defeats only naive LLM-first triage; YARA rules, entropy checks, AST parsing, and behavioral analysis still work.

title: Improving health intelligence in ChatGPT
blurb: OpenAI released GPT-5.5 Instant, which it says now performs comparably to its frontier Thinking models on its most challenging health evaluations, including HealthBench and HealthBench Professional. The model improves at recognizing when urgent care is needed, asking for relevant context, and explaining uncertainty, and is available to all free ChatGPT users, who send over 230 million health questions weekly.

title: Noam Shazeer Joins OpenAI
blurb: Noam Shazeer announced he is leaving Google to join OpenAI, calling it a difficult decision after years building the team there. Shazeer co-authored the original Transformer paper and co-founded Character.AI before returning to Google in 2024 to help lead Gemini.

title: UltraQuant: 4-bit KV Caching for Context-Heavy Agents
blurb: UltraQuant is a 4-bit KV-cache compression method for context-heavy agents, combining Walsh-Hadamard rotation, asymmetric K/V treatment, and an FP4 path with FP8 queries and native scaled-MFMA support on AMD CDNA4 GPUs.
