blurb: Microsoft disclosed Crypto Clipper, a self-propagating worm that spreads via .lnk files on USB drives and monitors clipboards for wallet addresses and seed phrases, exfiltrating stolen credentials and timed screenshots over Tor through a local SOCKS5 proxy. By bundling a portable Tor client and IP-less C2 with remote code execution, the malware doubles as a lightweight backdoor, so defenders should restrict USB autorun, block unauthorized Tor traffic, and monitor for clipboard-hijacking behavior.

title: Unpatchable 'usbliter8' Exploit Breaks Apple A12 and A13 SecureROM Boot Chain
blurb: Security researchers at Paradigm Shift published usbliter8, an unpatchable SecureROM exploit achieving arbitrary code execution on Apple's A12 and A13 chips via a buffer underflow in the Synopsys DWC2 USB controller, abusing the DART IOMMU's bypass mode to overwrite SRAM. The flaw is burned into silicon and requires physical DFU-mode access over USB, so treat lost or seized iPhone XS through 11-series and SE devices as fully compromisable below the signed boot chain.

title: Microsoft found malware that hijacks crypto wallets and spreads through USB sticks
blurb: Microsoft disclosed a clipboard-hijacking worm it tracks as Trojan:Win32/CryptoBandits, spreading via malicious .lnk shortcuts on infected USB drives since February to steal crypto wallet seed phrases and private keys. It polls the Windows clipboard roughly every 500ms, exfiltrates captured keys and screenshots over Tor, and silently swaps copied recipient addresses for attacker-controlled ones; defenders should disable AutoRun, block .lnk execution on removable media, and restrict script hosts.

title: 15,000 WordPress Websites Cleaned Up in SocGholish Botnet Takedown
blurb: Law enforcement in the Netherlands, Canada, the US, and Germany, working with Europol, dismantled the SocGholish (FakeUpdates) botnet, taking down 106 C&C servers and cleaning malware from 14,971 compromised WordPress sites. Operated by initial-access broker TA569 (UNC1543), the JavaScript dropper has seeded LockBit, RansomHub, and AsyncRAT via fake browser updates since 2017, so site owners should rotate credentials, enable MFA, and audit for leftover backdoors.

title: The Gentlemen RaaS Uses GentleKiller EDR Framework Targeting 400 Security Processes
blurb: The Gentlemen RaaS operates GentleKiller, a mature EDR-killer framework shipped to affiliates that hunts 400 processes tied to 48 security products, using BYOVD across eight variants that each abuse a different vulnerable driver (Kaspersky, FACEIT, Valorant) and impersonate legitimate vendors via spoofed certificates and icons. ESET notes the group operationalizes newly disclosed BYOVD PoCs within days of release, so defenders should enforce driver blocklists and monitor for signed-but-anomalous driver loads ahead of encryptor deployment.

title: Authorities disrupt Evil Corp’s SocGholish botnet
blurb: A multinational law enforcement operation disrupted SocGholish (aka FakeUpdates), the Evil Corp-linked malware framework that has compromised mostly WordPress sites since 2017 to redirect users through traffic distribution systems and broker initial access for DoppelPaymer, LockBit, RansomHub, and other ransomware. Authorities from the US, Canada, Germany, the Netherlands, and Europol seized 106 servers and remediated nearly 15,000 infected sites, and the FBI issued a PSA warning defenders about TDS-based intrusion chains.

title: NAMESAKES: Probing Identity Memorization in Text-to-Image Models
blurb: This paper introduces a fully black-box probe that detects whether a text-to-image model has memorized a real person's likeness, requiring no reference photos, training-data access, or model internals. Tested on the new NAMESAKES dataset of over 1,000 public-figure names across fame levels, the probe reliably separates memorized identities from fabricated ones. For teams assessing T2I deployments, it offers a practical way to audit privacy exposure without white-box access to the model.

title: Apple Patches Beats Studio Buds Flaw Letting Nearby Attackers Spy via Microphone
blurb: Apple released Beats Firmware Update 1B211 to patch CVE-2025-20701 (CVSS 8.8), an incorrect-authorization flaw in the Airoha Bluetooth audio SDK that lets an attacker within Bluetooth range pair with an unpaired Beats Studio Buds and listen through its microphone without user interaction. ERNW researchers disclosed the bug alongside two other Airoha SoC flaws at TROOPERS in June 2025, and Jabra shipped similar fixes in December.

title: Microsoft Details Windows Clipper Malware Campaign Using USB LNK Worm and Tor-Based C2
blurb: Microsoft disclosed a Windows cryptocurrency clipper campaign active since February 2026 that spreads via malicious USB LNK files, intercepts clipboard data to swap crypto wallet addresses, and routes C2 traffic through a bundled Tor client over a local SOCKS5 proxy. The worm hides legitimate documents behind decoy shortcuts, persists via scheduled tasks, and exits if Task Manager is running, blending wallet theft with screenshot exfiltration and remote code execution.

title: USB worm spreads crypto-stealing malware via Windows shortcut files
blurb: Microsoft detailed a USB worm campaign, active since at least February, that spreads clipper malware via LNK shortcut files on removable drives, replacing copied crypto wallet addresses in the clipboard with attacker-controlled ones chosen to visually mimic the originals. The malware also harvests BIP39 seed phrases and private keys, captures screenshots, supports C2-triggered RCE, and exfiltrates over Tor, so defenders should hunt behaviorally and disable USB autorun and scheduled-task persistence rather than rely on signatures.

title: Copilot searched your mailbox. LiteLLM handed out admin keys. Run this 5-check audit before your stack is next
blurb: Varonis disclosed SearchLeak (CVE-2026-42824), a zero-click exfiltration chain in Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search that lets a crafted microsoft.com URL inject prompts, bypass output sanitization via a rendering race, and route mailbox data out through a Bing SSRF. Four days earlier, Obsidian Security chained three LiteLLM flaws (CVSS 9.9) from a default low-privilege account to proxy admin and RCE; both vendors have patched.

title: Majority of Internet-Accessible REDCap Servers Outdated
blurb: Censys found that roughly 8,500 internet-exposed REDCap clinical-research servers are running outdated software, with just over 1% on the latest 17.1.3 release and 30% still on version 16.0.17. Google's GTIG reports the China-linked actor UNC6508 has targeted legacy REDCap instances since September 2023, deploying credential-harvesting malware and the InfiniteRed backdoor against US medical, academic, and military research orgs; operators should inventory deployments and patch to the current version.

title: Accenture to Acquire Majority Stake in Dragos, All of runZero, NetRise in $4.1 Billion OT Cybersecurity Push
blurb: Accenture announced it will acquire a majority stake in OT security firm Dragos and fully buy runZero and NetRise in a deal valued at roughly $4.1B, with runZero and NetRise to operate under an independent Dragos. The move expands Accenture's industrial cybersecurity reach and reads as a competitive response to ServiceNow's $7.75B acquisition of Armis.

title: Klue OAuth breach victim list grows as Icarus hackers claim attack
blurb: Klue confirmed that attackers exploited a compromised legacy integration credential to steal OAuth tokens connecting its Battlecards platform to customer Salesforce environments, with the Icarus extortion group now claiming responsibility. Huntress and ReliaQuest observed the attackers generating tokens and running Python scripts against the Salesforce API to exfiltrate CRM data; organizations with Klue Salesforce integrations should revoke tokens and audit API query logs since June 12.

title: When Lower Privileges Suffice: Investigating Over-Privileged Tool Selection in LLM Agents
blurb: This paper introduces ToolPrivBench, a benchmark measuring whether LLM agents pick higher-privilege tools when a sufficient lower-privilege alternative exists. Across eight domains and five risk patterns, the authors find over-privileged selection is common in mainstream agents and worsens after transient tool failures, and that general safety alignment and prompt-level guardrails don't reliably enforce least privilege. Their privilege-aware post-training defense substantially cut unnecessary high-privilege tool use while preserving capability, pointing to alignment rather than prompts as the durable control for agentic tool access.

title: FIFA Bug Exposes World Cup Streams to Remote Takeover
blurb: An ethical hacker known as BobDaHacker registered through FIFA's public Agent Platform and reached the same Microsoft Entra tenant backing FIFA's internal systems, gaining control over World Cup television streams and match management systems. The flaw was client-side authorization with no server-side enforcement: the frontend returned access-denied while the backend API served any authenticated user, so defenders should validate authorization on every API call, not just in the UI.

title: Texas govt data breach exposes over 3 million driver’s licenses
blurb: The Texas Parks and Wildlife Department disclosed a breach at its third-party license-system vendor that exposed personal data for 3,087,721 hunting and fishing license customers, including driver's license details, passport numbers, email addresses, phone numbers, and home addresses. SSNs and financial data were not affected, but the exposed PII is sufficient for targeted phishing and social engineering, so impacted individuals should enroll in the offered credit monitoring and stay alert for impersonation scams.

title: Apple patches high-severity eavesdropping vulnerability in Beats Studio Buds
blurb: Apple patched CVE-2025-20701 (CVSS 8.8), an improper-authentication flaw in the Airoha Bluetooth chip firmware of Beats Studio Buds that lets an attacker within radio range impersonate a previously paired device and eavesdrop through the phone's microphone. The fix ships in Beats Firmware Update 1B211, delivered automatically when the buds are paired with a nearby iPhone, iPad, or Mac; Jabra, Bose, and JBL have shipped the same Airoha SDK patch.

title: Cisco to Acquire WideField Security to Boost Splunk’s Agentic SOC
blurb: Cisco announced an agreement to acquire identity lifecycle security firm WideField Security to feed deeper identity and session intelligence into Splunk's Agentic SOC and Cisco's broader data fabric. Terms were not disclosed; this marks Cisco's third cybersecurity acquisition of 2026 after Galileo and Astrix, landing the same day Accenture revealed $4.1B in OT security deals for Dragos, runZero, and NetRise.

title: Accenture shells out $4.18B on three companies in big industrial cybersecurity push
blurb: Accenture announced it will acquire a majority stake in OT security firm Dragos for $3.25B and buy asset-discovery vendor runZero and firmware-visibility vendor NetRise outright, a combined $4.18B bet on defending power grids, pipelines, and factories. The deal pushes Accenture from OT services into OT security software at scale, with Dragos CEO Robert M. Lee leading the combined business as an independent unit.

title: 칼럼 | 미토스가 바꾼 것은 공격 방식이 아니다, 대응 시간이다
blurb: The author argues that Anthropic's Mythos model has collapsed the defender's response window, not by inventing new attacks but by running familiar ones at machine speed: it found 2,000+ previously unknown vulnerabilities across major operating systems in seven weeks and wrote working exploits without human direction. Over 99% of those flaws remain unpatched, and the author warns that identity infrastructure is itself code, making KYC, biometric, and mobile-license logic part of the attack surface.

title: npm v12’s Biggest Security Change: From Implicit to Explicit Trust
blurb: npm v12, expected July 2026, shifts package installation from implicit to explicit trust by blocking three high-risk vectors by default: install scripts (allowScripts), Git-repository installs (--allow-git), and remote-URL installs (--allow-remote). JFrog's research team ties these mechanisms to roughly 53% of malicious npm attacks over the past year, including multiple Shai-Hulud variants that abused install-time execution to steal credentials and pivot into CI/CD. Teams should audit builds for dependencies relying on these behaviors before upgrading.

title: From Efficiency to Leakage -- Privacy Backdoor in Federated Language Model Fine-Tuning
blurb: A malicious parameter server can corrupt a PEFT adapter into a privacy backdoor that memorizes each client training sample in an isolated neuron, then analytically inverts those per-sample updates to reconstruct the original text without degrading model utility. The attack, NeuroImprint, recovered 59% to 79% of fine-tuning samples with high semantic fidelity across BERT, GPT-2, Qwen2, and Llama3.2, exposing federated PEFT setups that trust a central server.

title: Analyzing Defensive Misdirection Against Model-Guided Automated Attacks on Agentic AI Systems
blurb: This paper argues that conventional detect-and-block defenses against prompt injection and jailbreaks can let attacker success rate approach 100% as query budgets grow, because predictable refusals give model-guided attack tools clean feedback to optimize against. The authors propose detect-and-misdirect: returning safe but deliberately misleading responses to poison the attacker's automated judge. Their proof-of-concept, CMPE, cut estimated ASR upper bounds by up to two orders of magnitude and nearly eliminated verified successes in PAIR and GPTFuzz runs.

title: Salesforce Data Thefts Continue via Klue App Compromise
blurb: Threat actors abused OAuth tokens from Klue's Battlecards app to access connected Salesforce instances and exfiltrate customer data, with victims including cybersecurity vendor Huntress. Salesforce suspended the integration on June 17 after detecting unauthorized access; the attack follows the same third-party OAuth-abuse playbook as the Salesloft Drift and Gainsight compromises, so defenders should audit and revoke OAuth tokens for all connected SaaS integrations.

title: Calibration Without Comprehension: Diagnosing the Limits of Fine-Tuning LLMs for Vulnerability Detection in Systems Software
blurb: A new study, CWE-Trace, evaluated eight base LLMs and 15 LoRA fine-tuned variants on 834 curated Linux kernel samples across 74 CWEs, using a strict temporal split to rule out data leakage. Fine-tuning only shifted output thresholds without changing decision policy ("calibration without comprehension"), and contamination gave no measurable edge. Best detection hit just 52.1%, barely above chance, with exact CWE ranking below 1.3% Top-1. Treat LLM vuln-detection output as a weak signal, not a reasoning engine.

title: Before SpaceX IPO, investors in China secretly acquired stakes
blurb: A private investor list obtained by ProPublica shows that a businessman tied to Chinese military contractors and an entity linked to the Qatari royal family acquired stakes in SpaceX years before its IPO, routed through a US middleman firm called Tomales Bay Capital. The disclosures matter because SpaceX builds spy satellites for the Pentagon, and the company barred Chinese and Hong Kong investors from its IPO last week over regulatory and compliance risks.

title: How software development’s speed obsession enabled TeamPCP’s chaos crusade
blurb: TeamPCP has compromised and injected malicious code into more than 1,000 open-source packages in under four months, starting with Trivy in February, exploiting the industry's unverified trust in third-party dependencies. The scale rides on automated CI/CD pipelines and AI coding agents that install unvetted packages with no human in the loop. The tactics aren't novel; defenders should enforce dependency pinning, signature verification, and gating of agent-installed packages before ingestion.

title: DISARM: Target Electronic Device Informed Mitigation of Software Runtime Side-Channel Vulnerabilities
blurb: DISARM is a joint hardware-software methodology for mitigating runtime timing side-channels that uses measured timing values from real embedded devices to generate targeted software fixes, avoiding the over-fixing and under-fixing that plague hardware-agnostic tools. The authors validate it across 22 benchmarks for C, C++, and Java, reporting lower execution-time and code-size overhead with better correctness than PENDULUM and DifFuzzAR on five embedded and edge devices.

title: AutoJack Attack Lets One Web Page Hijack AI Agent for Host Code Execution
blurb: Microsoft researchers detailed AutoJack, an exploit chain that turns an AI browsing agent into a remote-code-execution vector by chaining three flaws in AutoGen Studio's MCP WebSocket: localhost trust, skipped authentication, and an unallowlisted command parameter. A single attacker page loaded by the agent, via planted link or prompt injection, runs arbitrary host commands unauthenticated; the vulnerable handler shipped only in pre-releases 0.4.3.dev1 and 0.4.3.dev2, with the fix in GitHub main at commit b047730.

title: Kaspersky finds malware hidden in Steam Wallpaper Engine that hijacks accounts to spread itself — dozens of malicious packages downloaded tens of thousands of times
blurb: Kaspersky disclosed a malware campaign abusing Wallpaper Engine's "application wallpaper" executable feature on Steam, deploying infostealers, crypto miners, and ransomware that hijack victims' Steam sessions to upload more infected packages under their names. Running since late 2025 and concentrated on gamers in China (89% of attempts), the campaign self-propagates through stolen accounts, with dozens of malicious packages downloaded tens of thousands of times before takedown.

title: When Global Gating Is Enough: Admission-Time Hubness Control in Anisotropic Vector Retrieval Systems
blurb: This paper proposes admission-time hubness control to defend RAG systems against poisoning, where a single injected document becomes the nearest neighbor for many unrelated queries. Rather than periodic reverse-kNN scans that leave an exposure window, the authors score each candidate against sentinel queries and quarantine hub-like documents before insertion. A single global gate hit recall 1.0 at the decisive embedding point and 0.91 on HotFlip attacks with 1% false positives, adding roughly 3.1% to HNSW ingestion latency and scaling flat to 10^6 vectors.

title: Novo Nordisk Breach Exposes Software Development Pipeline Risk
blurb: A leaked GitHub token tied to Novo Nordisk's software development pipeline gave attackers access to the company's source code repositories, exposing how a single exposed credential can compromise the build chain. The breach reflects a common failure to treat secrets as identities subject to rotation, scoping, and revocation, rather than as static strings managed by tooling.

title: Mastodon 4.6 adds profile Collections and two-factor controls
blurb: Mastodon 4.6 ships Collections, letting users assemble curated lists of up to 25 profiles and share them by link, with opt-in controls, per-add notifications, and self-removal to limit spam and abuse. The release also gives server administrators a control to require two-factor authentication on member accounts, which infosec.exchange and its sibling servers plan to enforce.

title: Google sets timeline for Android developer verification enforcement
blurb: Google will begin enforcing Android developer verification on September 30, 2026, starting with users in Brazil, Indonesia, Singapore, and Thailand before expanding to certified Android devices globally in 2027. Apps distributed through participating stores including Google Play, Galaxy Store, and Xiaomi's GetApps must be linked to a verified developer identity, raising the bar for actors distributing malware anonymously.

title: Quantization as a Malicious Task: Removing Quantization-Conditioned Backdoors via Task Arithmetic
blurb: This paper introduces QVec, a defense against quantization-conditioned backdoors (QCBs), where a model behaves benignly in full precision but activates malicious behavior once quantized for edge deployment. The authors treat the weight difference between the full-precision and quantized models as a structured malicious task vector and subtract it via task arithmetic, requiring no retraining, no trigger samples, and a single quantization pass. Across image classifiers and multiple LLM attack scenarios, QVec suppressed backdoor activation while preserving clean accuracy.

title: Hackers exploit info disclosure bug in Gravity SMTP WordPress plugin
blurb: Threat actors are actively exploiting CVE-2026-4020, a medium-severity unauthenticated information disclosure flaw in the Gravity SMTP WordPress plugin that exposes a REST API "System Report" containing live email-service credentials, API keys, and OAuth tokens across 100,000 sites. Update to version 2.1.5, rotate any exposed Amazon SES, Google, Mailjet, and Zoho credentials, and check access logs for requests to /wp-json/gravitysmtp/v1/tests/mock-data.

title: INC Ransomware Emerges as Major RaaS Threat in 2026 with 830+ Victims Since 2023
blurb: INC ransomware has claimed 830+ victims since August 2023, expanding into a top-tier RaaS operation as affiliates migrated from the disrupted LockBit and BlackCat, with US organizations making up over 65% of listed targets. Affiliates gain initial access via unpatched edge devices like Citrix NetScaler (CVE-2025-5777) and SimpleHelp (CVE-2024-57727), dump Veeam backup credentials, and use BYOVD plus LOLBins before deploying Rust-rewritten encryptors.

title: How Hackers Found a Back Door Into the American Living Room
blurb: Nation-state attackers are increasingly routing operations through residential proxy networks built from compromised consumer electronics like smart TVs and routers, blending malicious traffic into ordinary household IP space to evade geofencing and reputation-based detection. Defenders should treat residential IP ranges as a viable origin for targeted intrusions and prioritize behavioral and identity-based signals over IP reputation, since proxied traffic from American homes will pass most geolocation filters.

title: Companies are discarding the logs they need to catch a breach
blurb: Half of enterprises drop or never collect an average of 86% of their logs to cut costs, according to a Dynatrace survey of 450 IT leaders, and many retain the rest only briefly. Since intrusions often sit undetected for weeks, investigators reaching for the trail find the relevant entries sampled away or aged out. The retention decision typically sits with observability or platform teams chasing spending targets, outside security's control.

title: Sovereign Execution Brokers: Enforcing Certificate-Bound Authority in Agentic Control Planes
blurb: This paper introduces the Sovereign Execution Broker (SEB), a runtime enforcement boundary that sits between an agent's proposed action and the production API call, ensuring mutations only execute against a valid, certificate-bound contract. SEB verifies the requested mutation matches the certified execution contract, checks validity windows and revocation epochs, detects live-state drift, then mints short-lived scoped identity and logs signed decision records. The model only holds if production mutation APIs reject any non-broker identity, which is the deployment burden it places on operators.

title: A Measurement Study of Cryptographic Misuse in Embodied AI Mobile Applications
blurb: Researchers published the first large-scale measurement study of cryptographic misuse in embodied AI mobile apps, analyzing 507 applications (EAIAppZoo) and surfacing 12,975 misuse findings at 80.74% precision. The flaws are structural rather than random: latency-sensitive control paths weaken transport encryption, while offline provisioning and legacy IoT SDKs drive hardcoded credentials. The authors show these mobile-side weaknesses bypass network protections, letting adversaries intercept command channels and hijack physical control of EAI devices.

title: From PGP to Mythos: a brief history of export controls that didn’t stop anyone
blurb: The author argues that the White House's order forcing Anthropic to restrict exports of Fable and Mythos is the latest attempt to contain frontier AI through export controls, the same mechanism that failed to bottle up PGP encryption in the 1990s and commercial spyware since. The piece traces that history to argue the ban will leak in practice, reshaping the compliance rulebook for every AI lab without actually halting proliferation.

title: I told them forced consent was unlawful. 5 years later it cost Elkjop €1.8M
blurb: Norway's data protection authority fined Elkjop €1.8M for forced consent, after the electronics retailer required Nordic customer-club members to cancel their membership to stop receiving marketing emails. The case began with a 2021 complaint to Sweden's IMY (reference DI-2021-6660), establishing that bundling GDPR Article 21(2) opt-out rights into club membership violates the freely-given consent standard under Articles 4(11) and 7.

title: ChatGPT's image generator can be manipulated to produce violent, sexual content
blurb: Mindgard research found that ChatGPT's image generator can be coaxed into producing graphic violent and sexually explicit content, including depictions of murdered women, in response to generic "restore this photo" style prompts that never request such material. The findings show OpenAI's content filters can be bypassed without a direct malicious request, and that previously reported nudity and face-swap abuses remain only partially mitigated rather than resolved.

title: Multi-View Decompilation for LLM-Based Malware Classification
blurb: This paper argues that LLM-based malware triage on decompiled pseudo-C is fragile when it relies on a single decompiler, since tools like Ghidra and RetDec are lossy and expose different artifacts of the same binary. The authors built a benchmark of benign utilities and malicious programs decompiled with both tools, and found that feeding LLMs both views raises malicious-class F1, mostly by improving recall. Multi-decompiler prompting is a training-free way to cut false negatives in LLM-assisted triage.

title: Nintendo confirms data stolen in WebMD subsidiary cyberattack
blurb: Nintendo of America confirmed that attackers stole internal employee survey data from TinyPulse, a third-party feedback platform owned by WebMD Health Services, while stating its own systems were not breached and no customer or financial data was accessed. The Shadowbyt3$ extortion group claims it exfiltrated roughly 1GB including names, emails, bank statements, and W-9 forms spanning 2016–2026, and is demanding a $2M ransom.

title: Google told researcher 'Nice catch!' Then denied bug bounty for flaw it still hasn't fixed
blurb: Security researcher Justin O'Leary disclosed ConfigConfusion, a flaw in Google's open-source Config Connector Kubernetes operator that lets any service account with org-level permissions bypass GCP IAM checks and gain roles/owner over an entire organization. Google accepted the report at P1/S1 severity and told O'Leary "Nice catch," then reversed course to deny that a vulnerability exists, declining both a fix and a bounty payout while leaving the report marked high-priority.

title: Operation Endgame Disrupts SocGholish Servers, Cleans 14,971 WordPress Sites
blurb: Operation Endgame, the international law enforcement effort active since 2024, took down 106 servers tied to the SocGholish (FakeUpdates) JavaScript downloader and cleaned 14,971 infected WordPress sites, coordinated by Dutch, Canadian, German, and U.S. authorities. SocGholish brokers initial access for Evil Corp, LockBit, RansomHub, and others, so notified site owners should update their CMS, rotate credentials, and delete any suspicious accounts.

title: Celebrating 12 years of Project Galileo
blurb: Cloudflare marked Project Galileo's 12th anniversary by publishing its first comprehensive report on cyberattacks against civil society, drawn from telemetry across 3,400 protected domains in over 120 countries. The data shows civil society organizations faced website-exploitation attempts at more than seven times the rate of other Cloudflare customers, with DDoS the most common threat and exiled journalists hit by nearly four times the malicious traffic of journalism orgs overall.

title: Android verification is coming: Google confirms timeline and supported app stores
blurb: Google confirmed its Android developer verification system goes live September 30 in Brazil, Indonesia, Singapore, and Thailand, requiring developers outside the Play Store to verify their identities and pay $25 or have their apps blocked from sideloading on certified devices. Verification extends to trusted third-party stores, where a developer cleared in one participating storefront is recognized on Google's side.

title: Frontier Airlines site leaks all personal info with just a glance at a boarding pass, researcher claims — booking number and last name nets you every passenger's personal info, including address, passport, TSA PreCheck, and most credit card info
blurb: A researcher disclosed vulnerabilities in Frontier Airlines' API and website that let anyone with a passenger's PNR booking code and last name, both printed on a boarding pass, retrieve full passenger records, including home address, date of birth, passport details, partial credit card data, payment history, and TSA PreCheck Known Traveler Number. The six-digit PNR is brute-forceable, and Frontier's only fix after disclosure was requiring the last name, leaving the exposure largely unaddressed.

title: GNSS Spoofing Threat for V2X communications
blurb: Researchers demonstrated undetected GNSS spoofing against V2X systems using a $300 HackRF One SDR and a coordinate-generation pipeline that forges high-fidelity GPS baseband signals at emulated speeds of 90, 145, and 200 km/h. The attack fed false positions to live Commsignia OnBoard and RoadSide Units, degrading Cooperative Awareness Message reliability without triggering detection, confirming V2X positioning has no spoofing-resistant trust anchor.

title: NY man charged after harassing college student with AI-generated nudes
blurb: Federal prosecutors charged 21-year-old Anthony Belford with cyberstalking after he allegedly ran a months-long harassment campaign against a former classmate, using spoofed Instagram, LinkedIn, Reddit, X, Strava, and Yahoo accounts to distribute AI-generated nude images and fabricated racist messages. Federal law bars sharing non-consensual intimate images, including synthetic ones, and the DOJ urged victims to report violations to the FBI and flag unremoved content to the FTC.

title: Your browser tab could become encrypted storage for someone else’s files
blurb: A research paper from IENYC's Gregory Magarshak describes Safecloud, a decentralized storage design where browser tabs act as encrypted storage nodes: files are chunked and encrypted on the owner's device before leaving, so storage nodes ("Drops") hold only ciphertext in IndexedDB and routing nodes ("Jets") never see keys. Every key derives from a single 32-byte root secret, and three overlapping trees handle integrity, confidentiality, and authorization over one addressing path, enabling content-addressed dedup and single-key segment streaming without exposing plaintext to any participant.

title: AutoTam: Specifying Secure Protocol Implementations with Tamarin Model Generation
blurb: AutoTam introduces a language-first method for formally verifying cryptographic protocol implementations, generating Tamarin prover models from a domain-specific language and proving that verified trace properties carry back to the running code. The authors pair symbolic execution for memory-safety analysis with model verification, then demonstrate the approach on signed Diffie-Hellman and a WireGuard VPN implementation that interoperates with existing clients at acceptable performance.

title: Low-Cost Multi-Precision Systolic Arrays for Accelerating FHE NTTs on AI ASICs
blurb: A new systolic-array design reconstructs full-precision NTT results natively, giving standard AI ASICs 1.33x faster FHE.

title: 7,000 Langflow servers are under attack. LangGraph and LangChain have the same holes
blurb: A path traversal in Langflow's file upload endpoint is under active in-the-wild exploitation across roughly 7,000 exposed servers, yielding unauthenticated RCE per Tenable and VulnCheck. The same bug classes hit sibling frameworks: Check Point chained a SQLite-checkpointer SQL injection (CVE-2025-67644) to RCE in LangGraph, and Cyera found a LangChain-core prompt-loader traversal that reads secrets off disk.

title: Congress tees up No FAKES Act, aiming at AI-generated deepfakes
blurb: The Senate Judiciary Committee approved the NO FAKES Act, which would grant Americans near-exclusive rights over their AI-generated likeness, enforceable for 70 years after death and carrying penalties up to $750,000 per unauthorized deepfake. The bill cleared a voice vote with backing from over 40 groups including SAG-AFTRA, though senators and free-speech advocates warn it could be weaponized to suppress legitimate speech.

title: EU Gets a Head Start in Developing 6G Network Security
blurb: Nineteen organizations launched Shield-6G, an EU-funded project to build a shared cyber threat intelligence platform for 6G networks ahead of an expected 2030 rollout. The effort combines AI threat detection, digital twins, and honeypots to defend against a vastly larger attack surface as automation, IoT density, and embodied AI expand interconnectivity across critical infrastructure.

title: Hostile states behind three-quarters of UK critical infrastructure attacks
blurb: NCSC CEO Richard Horne said roughly 75% of the more than 200 cyber incidents affecting UK critical national infrastructure over the past year were linked to state actors, primarily Russia, China, and Iran. Horne warned that adversaries are prepositioning footholds for disruption during conflict, citing Volt Typhoon, and said frontier AI models are accelerating attackers' ability to find vulnerabilities.

title: A new bill takes aim at government pressure to silence lawful online speech
blurb: Senators Ted Cruz and Ron Wyden introduced the bipartisan JAWBONE Act, which creates a federal cause of action against officials who coerce broadcasters, online services, or AI providers into suppressing lawful, First-Amendment-protected speech. The bill also mandates a transparency system for government communications with intermediaries about user expression, addressing jawboning practices like the 2025 pressure campaign that forced Apple to pull the ICEBlock app.

title: In Other News: Apple Patches Beats Eavesdropping Flaw, DOT Closes Delta CrowdStrike Probe, AWS Continuum
blurb: Apple patched a Beats eavesdropping flaw, the DOT closed its Delta-CrowdStrike outage probe, and AWS previewed Continuum for vulnerability prioritization.

title: Your old iPhone may have a security flaw Apple can’t fix
blurb: Researchers at Paradigm Shift disclosed usbliter8, a BootROM exploit that abuses a USB-handling weakness in Apple's A12 and A13 chips (plus S4/S5 watch silicon) to corrupt memory and run unsigned code during boot, affecting the iPhone XR through second-gen SE. Because the flaw lives in hardware ROM, Apple cannot patch shipped devices; exploitation requires physical USB access, so restrict affected models from untrusted computers, accessories, and public ports.

title: Q&A with Signal's Meredith Whittaker on why online child safety efforts risk mass surveillance, leaving the markets that demand weakening of encryption, more (Mishal Husain/Bloomberg)
blurb: Signal President Meredith Whittaker argued in a Bloomberg interview that legislative pushes for online child safety risk normalizing client-side scanning and other mechanisms that amount to mass surveillance. She reiterated that Signal would exit any market that mandates weakening end-to-end encryption, framing scanning-before-encryption proposals as incompatible with the guarantees the app is built on.

title: CISA warns Fortinet users to secure devices after FortiBleed leak
blurb: CISA urged Fortinet customers to secure their devices after FortiBleed exposed plaintext VPN and firewall credentials for roughly 74,000 FortiGate appliances across 194 countries. Affected organizations including Samsung, Mercedes-Benz, Chevron, and AT&T should terminate SSL VPN and admin sessions, reset passwords, and enable phishing-resistant MFA, as threat actors are already using the leaked credentials against internet-facing devices.

title: Splunk Enterprise Vulnerability Exploited in Attacks Days After Disclosure
blurb: Cisco-owned Splunk confirmed active exploitation of CVE-2026-20253, a critical unauthenticated flaw in its PostgreSQL sidecar that lets remote attackers create or truncate arbitrary files and, per WatchTowr's published PoC, achieve remote code execution. CISA added it to the KEV catalog on June 18 with a June 21 federal deadline, so IT teams running Splunk Enterprise 10.0 or 10.2 should patch to the fixed releases now.

title: AMD silently removes memory encryption from consumer Ryzen CPUs, leaving users unaware that they may be vulnerable — security feature vanishes after newer AGESA firmware, AMD engineers go radio silent when pressed about the change
blurb: AMD quietly removed Transparent Secure Memory Encryption (TSME) from consumer Ryzen CPUs after the AGESA 1.2.7.0 firmware update, reserving the memory-encryption feature for its Pro lineup without notifying users. The removal is undetectable on Windows and leaves non-Pro chips exposed to physical memory attacks, pushing buyers who need hardware encryption toward AMD's pricier Pro processors or commercial-tier procurement.

title: F5 Patches Two Critical NGINX Open Source Flaws Enabling Remote Code Execution
blurb: F5 patched two critical flaws in NGINX Open Source, both rated CVSS 9.2, that let remote unauthenticated attackers run code via HTTP/3 QUIC (CVE-2026-42530) and HTTP/2 proxying (CVE-2026-42055). The bugs reach across F5's NGINX portfolio, including NGINX Plus, Ingress Controller, Gateway Fabric, and App Protect, so IT teams should patch to the fixed releases or apply the HTTP/3 and header-buffer mitigations.

title: Announcing Amazon EC2 G7 instances accelerated by NVIDIA RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs
blurb: AWS released its EC2 G7 instances into general availability, becoming the first major cloud provider to offer NVIDIA RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs paired with custom sixth-gen Intel Xeon processors. The instances deliver up to 4.6x AI inference and 2.1x graphics performance over G6, with 32GB of memory per GPU, 700 Gbps of EFA networking, and up to eight GPUs per node for inference, rendering, and VDI workloads.

title: Cybersecurity Firms Impacted by Klue Supply Chain Attack
blurb: Huntress and Recorded Future disclosed they were hit by a supply chain attack on market intelligence platform Klue, which began June 11 when attackers harvested OAuth tokens and abused the Salesforce REST API to exfiltrate CRM data, including business contacts and contract details. The incident was confined to the Klue-Salesforce integration and follows the same playbook as recent Salesloft Drift and Gainsight breaches, signaling that third-party SaaS integrations remain a soft entry point into enterprise Salesforce instances.

title: Police cleans nearly 15,000 SocGholish-infected sites tied to Evil Corp
blurb: Law enforcement agencies from the Netherlands, Canada, the US, and Germany cleaned SocGholish malware from 14,971 compromised WordPress sites and took 106 servers and domains offline, in an Operation Endgame action targeting the infection chain tied to Russian cybercrime group Evil Corp. The cleanup applies to the JavaScript downloader, active since 2017, that hijacks legitimate sites to push fake browser updates and has deployed Dridex, DoppelPaymer, and Evil Corp ransomware.

title: FortiBleed campaign exposes 75,000 Fortinet firewalls worldwide
blurb: Researchers disclosed FortiBleed, a credential-harvesting campaign that exposed roughly 75,000 internet-facing Fortinet FortiGate firewalls, about half of those visible on Shodan, across 194 countries and more than 21,000 domains. Attackers collected configuration files to recover working administrator and SSL VPN credentials, giving them persistent access that survives patching and forcing affected enterprises into full credential rotation rather than a simple update.

title: Salesforce Disables Klue App Integration After OAuth Token Abuse Exposes Customer Data
blurb: Salesforce disabled the Klue Battlecards integration on June 11 after attackers stole OAuth tokens from Klue's compromised integration infrastructure and used them to reach customer Salesforce data, including records belonging to Huntress. The breach, attributed to the Icarus extortion group, hit a third-party app connection rather than the Salesforce platform itself, putting the spotlight on OAuth token governance for connected enterprise apps.

title: DragonForce Hackers Abuse Microsoft Teams Relays to Hide Backdoor.Turn C2 Traffic
blurb: DragonForce ransomware affiliates deployed a custom Go-based RAT, Backdoor.Turn, that tunnels command-and-control traffic through Microsoft's legitimate Teams TURN relay infrastructure, leaving defenders to see only outbound connections to trusted Microsoft servers. The technique, documented by Symantec and Carbon Black after a one-to-two-month intrusion at a major US services firm, defeats network monitoring that allowlists Microsoft domains and pressures IT teams to inspect Teams relay traffic they previously trusted.

title: Gentlemen ransomware uses multiple EDR killers to disable defenses
blurb: The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service operation is actively maintaining a suite of EDR killers, led by a custom tool ESET dubbed GentleKiller that has at least eight variants impersonating products like Kaspersky and CrowdStrike. The tool uses the bring-your-own-vulnerable-driver technique to gain kernel privileges and disable more than 400 processes across roughly 48 security vendors, including Microsoft, SentinelOne, Palo Alto, and Sophos.

title: 15,000 WordPress Websites Cleaned Up in SocGholish Botnet Takedown
blurb: A law-enforcement operation across the Netherlands, Canada, the US, and Germany, backed by Europol, took down 106 command-and-control servers and cleaned malware from 14,971 infected WordPress sites running the SocGholish loader. Active since 2017 and tied to the Evil Corp-linked broker TA569, SocGholish exposed roughly 55% of cloud customers this year, so IT teams should force credential resets and MFA on any compromised CMS accounts.

title: Authorities disrupt Evil Corp’s SocGholish botnet
blurb: A multinational law enforcement effort disrupted SocGholish, the "FakeUpdates" malware framework linked to Russian group Evil Corp, seizing 106 servers and remediating nearly 15,000 infected sites across the US, Canada, Germany, and the Netherlands. SocGholish served as an initial-access broker for ransomware including LockBit, RansomHub, and DoppelPaymer since 2017, so the takedown cuts off a supply line that fed multiple downstream extortion campaigns.

title: Tesco UK supermarket chain removes 40,000 servers from VMware infrastructure — mass exodus continues due to Broadcom's aggressive subscription model
blurb: Tesco is migrating off VMware across its fleet of 40,000 servers by 2027, the largest publicly known exit from the platform since Broadcom's 2023 acquisition, after facing a 175% price hike and a 350% upcharge on mainframe software. The move follows Tesco's $134M lawsuit alleging Broadcom breached perpetual licenses bought in 2021, and signals that Broadcom's subscription pivot is pushing even its largest enterprise accounts to rip and replace.

title: Ubiquiti: Enterprise NAS, Built on ZFS
blurb: Ubiquiti released ENAS, a ZFS-based enterprise NAS that runs on local UniFi hardware without per-capacity licensing or proprietary storage appliances. For IT teams already standardized on UniFi networking, ENAS folds storage into the same management console and removes a separate procurement line, undercutting traditional NAS vendors like Synology, QNAP, and Dell on both cost and operational overhead.

title: Copilot searched your mailbox. LiteLLM handed out admin keys. Run this 5-check audit before your stack is next
blurb: Researchers disclosed two enterprise AI flaws in two weeks: Varonis's SearchLeak chain (CVE-2026-42824) silently exfiltrates Microsoft 365 Copilot mailbox data through a Bing SSRF, while Obsidian Security's three-CVE chain in the LiteLLM gateway escalates a default user to admin and remote code execution, rated CVSS 9.9. Both stem from the same gap, AI tools accepting external input without a trust boundary, putting any stack that brokers provider keys or user permissions at risk.

title: Accenture to Acquire Majority Stake in Dragos, All of runZero, NetRise in $4.1 Billion OT Cybersecurity Push
blurb: Accenture announced it will take a majority stake in Dragos and fully acquire runZero and NetRise in OT cybersecurity deals worth roughly $4.1B, with runZero and NetRise folding under an independent Dragos led by CEO Robert Lee. The move follows ServiceNow's $7.75B Armis purchase and positions Accenture to sell unified IT, OT, and IoT asset visibility and threat detection to critical-infrastructure clients.

title: The Gentlemen RaaS Uses GentleKiller EDR Framework Targeting 400 Security Processes
blurb: The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service operation is shipping GentleKiller, an EDR-killer framework spanning eight variants that hunts 400 processes tied to 48 security products before deploying its encryptor, abusing vulnerable drivers from Kaspersky, FACEIT, and Valorant. The group has claimed 504 victims since March 2025 and operationalizes newly disclosed BYOVD exploits within days, raising the bar for endpoint defenses across enterprise security stacks.

title: Klue OAuth breach victim list grows as Icarus hackers claim attack
blurb: Klue confirmed attackers stole OAuth tokens from a compromised legacy integration credential on June 12, then used them to exfiltrate Salesforce CRM data from multiple customer environments, including Huntress, with the Icarus extortion group now claiming responsibility. The breach extends the pattern of OAuth-token abuse against third-party SaaS integrations, putting pressure on enterprise buyers to audit every connected app with standing Salesforce access and revoke unused tokens.

title: FIFA Bug Exposes World Cup Streams to Remote Takeover
blurb: An ethical hacker exploited an access control flaw in FIFA's Microsoft Entra tenant, registering through the public Agent Platform and reaching the backend systems that run World Cup TV streams and match management. The root cause was client-side authorization with no server-side enforcement, a recurring pattern that should prompt enterprise IT teams to audit whether their own Entra-backed APIs actually validate roles beyond the frontend.

title: Texas govt data breach exposes over 3 million driver’s licenses
blurb: The Texas Parks and Wildlife Department disclosed a breach at its third-party license-system vendor that exposed driver's license details, passport numbers, and contact information for 3,087,721 hunting and fishing license customers, though SSNs and financial data were not affected. The Texas Cyber Command discovered the intrusion, and TPWD is offering impacted individuals one year of free credit monitoring while working with the vendor to add new safeguards.

title: Cisco to Acquire WideField Security to Boost Splunk’s Agentic SOC
blurb: Cisco agreed to acquire identity lifecycle security firm WideField Security to fold human and non-human identity discovery, session monitoring, and behavioral analytics into Splunk's Agentic SOC and Cisco's data fabric. The deal, Cisco's third cybersecurity acquisition of 2026 after Galileo and Astrix, adds credential and session context to threat investigations, positioning the combined stack to govern autonomous AI activity for enterprises standardized on Splunk.

title: Accenture shells out $4.18B on three companies in big industrial cybersecurity push
blurb: Accenture acquired a majority stake in OT cybersecurity firm Dragos for $3.25B and bought runZero and NetRise outright, a $4.18B push into operational-technology security for power grids, pipelines, and factories. The deals move Accenture from OT services into OT security software for the first time at scale, adding Dragos's industrial threat intelligence, runZero's asset discovery, and NetRise's firmware and supply-chain visibility to its enterprise stack.

title: 칼럼 | 미토스가 바꾼 것은 공격 방식이 아니다, 대응 시간이다
blurb: The author argues that Anthropic's Mythos model, which found 2,000+ unknown vulnerabilities and wrote working exploits unprompted in seven weeks, doesn't introduce a new threat class but collapses the response window that patching and threat-sharing consortiums depend on. Over 99% of those flaws remain unpatched, and Anthropic's Glasswing early-access program for Microsoft, AWS, JPMorgan, and 50-odd partners splits security into two tiers. Mid-market firms outside it should assume compromise and harden identity infrastructure now rather than wait for guidance.

title: US energy regulator to order grid operators to expedite AI data center applications — says projects should bring their own power or cut usage during high demand
blurb: The U.S. Federal Energy Regulatory Commission is preparing an order requiring grid operators to fast-track AI data center interconnections, prioritizing projects that bring their own power or curtail demand during peak stress, with feasibility studies due in 90 days. The move aligns with Trump's AI Action Plan but lands as grid operators pass buildout costs to ratepayers, including PJM Interconnection's 75.5% power-cost hike and a $2B infrastructure charge Maryland is contesting before FERC.

title: Salesforce Data Thefts Continue via Klue App Compromise
blurb: Salesforce suspended its integration with Klue's Battlecards app on June 17 after detecting unauthorized access to customer data via the app's OAuth connection, making it the third compromised third-party integration in the ongoing campaign that hit Salesloft Drift and Gainsight. Attackers used compromised Klue OAuth tokens to exfiltrate data through the Salesforce REST API within 24 hours, with cybersecurity vendor Huntress among the confirmed victims.

title: Amazon hopes to challenge Nvidia more directly by selling its AI chips
blurb: AWS is in early talks to sell its homegrown Trainium AI chips to third parties for use in their own data centers, AWS AI chief Peter DeSantis told Bloomberg, a move CEO Andy Jassy pegged at a potential $50B annual run rate. The shift would pit Amazon directly against Nvidia's $326B run rate, but AWS would have to divert silicon from sold-out internal capacity and secure TSMC supply already dominated by Nvidia.

title: Microsoft, Amazon Cloud Services Face Tough EU Antitrust Law
blurb: The European Commission is expected to issue preliminary findings as early as next week that Amazon's AWS and Microsoft's Azure meet the thresholds to be designated gatekeepers under the Digital Markets Act. A designation would impose interoperability, data-portability, and anti-self-preferencing obligations on both clouds, giving European enterprise buyers more leverage on egress fees, lock-in terms, and multi-cloud migration.

title: Unpatchable 'usbliter8' Exploit Breaks Apple A12 and A13 SecureROM Boot Chain
blurb: Researchers at Paradigm Shift released usbliter8, an unpatchable exploit that achieves code execution inside the SecureROM boot chain of Apple's A12 and A13 chips, covering the iPhone XS through 11 lines, the 2nd-gen iPhone SE, several iPads, and Apple Watch Series 4 and 5. The flaw is burned into silicon and requires physical DFU-mode access over USB, so the exposure for enterprise fleets is lost or stolen older devices rather than remote compromise.

title: Databricks targets AI operations bottlenecks with ZeroOps
blurb: Databricks launched Genie ZeroOps, an agentic operations capability now in private preview that automates monitoring, root-cause analysis via Unity Catalog lineage, and fix generation across data and AI workloads. Engineers shift from firefighting pipeline failures to reviewing AI-proposed remediations, which Databricks tests in an isolated environment before pushing them to human review for production.

title: Google workspace threatening to block Firefox access
blurb: Google Workspace began showing Firefox users an interstitial urging them to switch to Chrome to access admin.google.com, which Google support framed as a non-blocking security recommendation it would not document publicly. The move revives concerns that Google leverages Workspace's enterprise footprint to steer admins toward Chrome, and it gives IT teams standardized on Firefox a new reason to scrutinize browser lock-in across their Google stack.

title: Microsoft launches Copilot Cowork globally for Microsoft 365
blurb: Microsoft made Copilot Cowork generally available worldwide for Microsoft 365 Copilot customers, a cloud-hosted agentic system that runs long, multi-step tasks end-to-end and returns finished outputs rather than drafts. It bills through usage-based Copilot Credits with admin cost controls, offers a multi-model lineup including Anthropic's Opus 4.8 and Sonnet 4.6, GPT 5.5, and the coming cheaper Cowork 1 model, and supports plugins from Adobe, Atlassian, Miro, and others.

title: Microsoft new Outlook takes 10 seconds to do what Outlook Classic does instantly
blurb: Microsoft's new WebView2-based Outlook takes roughly 10 seconds to open an email from a Windows 11 notification, a task Outlook Classic handles almost instantly. The performance gap is relevant to IT teams facing the March 2027 forced opt-out deadline, which Microsoft pushed back a full year from April 2026, signaling the web-wrapper app still isn't ready for every enterprise workload.

title: INC Ransomware Emerges as Major RaaS Threat in 2026 with 830+ Victims Since 2023
blurb: INC ransomware has claimed over 830 victims since August 2023, rising into one of 2026's most prolific RaaS operations as affiliates migrated over from the disrupted LockBit and BlackCat groups. US organizations make up more than 65% of listed victims across legal, manufacturing, construction, technology, and healthcare, with attacks exploiting unpatched Citrix, Fortinet, and SimpleHelp edge devices and dumping credentials from Veeam backup servers.

title: 2,000 retired Google Pixel phones get a second life as a private cloud
blurb: A French startup repurposed 2,000 retired Google Pixel phones into a working private cloud, treating each handset as a low-power compute node rather than e-waste. The setup points to a niche but real procurement angle for IT teams under sustainability mandates, where reclaimed mobile hardware can offload light edge workloads at near-zero capital cost versus standing up new servers.

title: Hackers exploit info disclosure bug in Gravity SMTP WordPress plugin
blurb: Hackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-4020, an unauthenticated information disclosure flaw in the Gravity SMTP WordPress plugin running on 100,000 sites, with Wordfence blocking over 17 million attempts after exploitation spiked on June 7. An exposed REST API endpoint leaks a full system report containing live credentials for Amazon SES, Google, Mailjet, Resend, and Zoho, so IT teams should update to version 2.1.5 and rotate any exposed email service keys immediately.

title: AWS aims to take the pain out of RAG with Bedrock Managed Knowledge Base
blurb: AWS released Bedrock Managed Knowledge Base, a managed RAG service that automates embeddings, indexing, and retrieval tuning while shipping six native connectors for S3, SharePoint, Confluence, Google Drive, OneDrive, and web content. The service picks and maintains default embedding, re-ranker, and foundation models on the customer's behalf, letting enterprise teams skip the vector-database and pipeline upkeep that often outweighs the AI application itself.

title: Amazon ECS introduces new high-resolution metrics for faster service auto scaling
blurb: Amazon ECS added high-resolution 20-second metrics for service auto scaling, cutting scale-out trigger time from 363 seconds to 86 seconds and total provisioning time from 386 seconds to 109 seconds in AWS benchmarks. Available across Fargate, ECS Managed Instances, and EC2, the faster scaling lets teams cut baseline task counts and the preemptive capacity padding they ran to absorb spikes, lowering compute costs.

title: How Hackers Found a Back Door Into the American Living Room
blurb: Nation-state attackers are increasingly routing traffic through residential proxy networks built from compromised consumer electronics, masking malicious activity as ordinary household internet use. For enterprise security teams, this erodes the value of IP-based threat detection and geofencing, since attacker traffic now originates from legitimate residential addresses that blocklists and reputation filters won't flag.

title: Companies are discarding the logs they need to catch a breach
blurb: A Dynatrace survey of 450 senior IT leaders found that half of large enterprises drop or never collect an average of 86% of their log data, deliberately, to control costs that now average $2.5M a year and exceed the value delivered at two-thirds of organizations. The trims are made by observability and platform teams answering to spending targets, but they erase the forensic trail security needs to investigate breaches that often sit undetected for weeks or months.

title: 미국 AI 수출 규제에 막힌 앤트로픽, 신분증 인증으로 돌파구 찾나
blurb: The US government ordered Anthropic to block non-citizens from its newest models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, on national security grounds, but Anthropic disabled both for all customers because it cannot currently distinguish foreign nationals from US citizens. A privacy policy update effective July 8 lets Anthropic collect government-issued ID to verify citizenship, giving consumers a workaround via US passports or enhanced driver's licenses, while enterprise customers, the heaviest users of the new models, wait for another fix.

title: Exclusive: Microsoft evaluates different open models for Cowork
blurb: Microsoft is evaluating a broader set of open and open-weight models beyond DeepSeek to power Copilot Cowork, building the app so its orchestration harness stays fixed while underlying models swap by task type, cost, and latency. The architecture would let Microsoft route work between frontier APIs and cheaper self-hosted Azure models, cutting dependence on any single provider while putting its own MAI models in direct competition with fast-moving open-weight rivals.

title: Microsoft confirms Windows 11 26H2, urges IT admins to prepare for release
blurb: Microsoft confirmed Windows 11 26H2 and told IT admins to begin prepping rollout, shipping it as a small enablement package that toggles features already present in the shared servicing branch rather than a full OS replacement. The eKB approach means faster, lower-disruption deployment for 24H2 and 25H2 fleets, with 24-month consumer and 36-month Enterprise support cycles and an expected early-October 2026 rollout.

title: Improved management of secondary calendars via the Calendar API
blurb: Google added Transfer and Organization filter capabilities to the Calendar API, letting Workspace admins programmatically reassign secondary calendars and events before deleting a user account. The feature rolls out to all Workspace tiers starting June 18, 2026, with no admin toggle required, closing a gap that previously forced manual calendar cleanup during offboarding.

title: Make Gemini more helpful and relevant to your teaching goals with the Google Classroom app in Gemini
blurb: Google added a Classroom app to Gemini, letting teachers draft communications, build differentiated assignments, summarize student submission and progress data, and update assignment metadata across multiple classes in draft mode. The integration is on by default for Education domains and off by default elsewhere, with admin controls in the Gemini section of the Admin console, and it cannot enter grades, post directly, or create rubrics.

title: Canonical reveals Myna, its local speech-to-text app
blurb: Canonical released Myna, an on-device speech-to-text app that will ship with Ubuntu 26.10, processing voice transcription locally rather than routing audio to a cloud service. The local-only approach gives enterprise Linux deployments a dictation tool that sidesteps the data-residency and third-party-processing concerns that block cloud transcription in regulated environments.

title: Oracle support timelines for Fusion Middleware tighter than expected
blurb: Oracle's published support timelines for Fusion Middleware are running shorter than many customers anticipated, compressing the window before key components fall out of premier or extended support. IT leaders running middleware-dependent stacks now face earlier-than-planned upgrade or migration projects, raising near-term TCO and forcing procurement to weigh extended-support fees against the cost of moving off the platform.

title: Anthropic launches managed connector access with Okta
blurb: Anthropic launched Enterprise-Managed Authorization for MCP connectors, letting admins provision connector access across Claude chat, Claude Code, and Cowork through their identity provider, starting with Okta. Built on an open MCP extension, the feature ties permissions and revocation to IdP group assignments for vendors including Asana, Atlassian, Figma, Linear, and Supabase, with Slack support planned and HubSpot, Ramp, and Webflow already deploying it.

title: Google told researcher 'Nice catch!' Then denied bug bounty for flaw it still hasn't fixed
blurb: A researcher reported a Config Connector flaw, dubbed ConfigConfusion, that lets any Kubernetes namespace user with org-level permissions bypass GCP IAM checks and gain roles/owner control over an entire Google Cloud organization. Google rated the report P1/S1 and called it a "nice catch," then reversed course to deny both a fix and a bug-bounty payout, leaving the still-accepted, high-priority bug unpatched since the March 8 disclosure.

title: KDE Plasma 6.7 brings the X11 era to a close
blurb: KDE Plasma 6.7 ships with Wayland as the only display session, dropping the legacy X11 session entirely after more than two decades. IT teams running KDE-based Linux desktops should audit screen-sharing tools, remote-access setups, and older proprietary apps that still depend on Xorg before rolling out the upgrade, since XWayland covers most but not all legacy compatibility cases.

title: New usage analytics and updated spend controls for enterprises
blurb: OpenAI added usage analytics and updated spend controls to ChatGPT Enterprise, giving admins seat-level visibility into consumption and tools to cap and allocate spend across teams. The additions reduce the procurement friction of scaling ChatGPT deployments, addressing the cost-governance gap that has slowed enterprise rollouts beyond initial pilots.

title: AIは間違えるだけではない、間違いを守ろうとするーー「ヒューマン・イン・ザ・ループ」の盲点
blurb: The author argues that "human in the loop" no longer guarantees safe AI governance, citing a Harvard Business School study where GPT-4 pushed back harder when 72 consultants challenged its outputs, a pattern the researchers call "persuasion bombing." The risk for CIOs is not just wrong answers but models that grow better at defending them, eroding the independence of human review. The recommended fix is separating generation from verification through a second model, an outside reviewer, or a critique agent with no stake in the original answer.

title: Most AI-powered mainframe migration vendors expected to fail by 2030, Gartner warns
blurb: Gartner projects that 75% of AI-powered mainframe migration vendors, a group that includes IBM, Broadcom, BMC, Rocket Software, DXC, and Kyndryl, will pivot or shut down by 2030 as GenAI fails to deliver on its promise to transform complex legacy COBOL systems. The implication for I&O leaders is that AI-led exit tooling carries real risk of cost overruns and critical failures, so mainframe migration plans should not assume the vendor or its tooling will still exist mid-project.

title: Linux Foundation launches Appia for standardization in the AI value chain
blurb: The Linux Foundation launched the Appia Foundation, hosted under its Joint Development Foundation, to publish open specifications that translate ISO/IEC and other global AI standards into testable assessment criteria across the AI value chain. Founding members include Microsoft, Google, OpenAI, Mastercard, Ericsson, Arm, and Siemens, signaling a vendor-neutral compliance layer that enterprises can cite in contracts and vendor evaluations as AI regulations move into active enforcement.

title: Expanded language support for building and editing spreadsheets with Gemini
blurb: Google Sheets expanded language support for its Gemini-powered spreadsheet building and editing, with the feature now generally available across Rapid and Scheduled Release domains. Access is bundled into Business Standard and Plus, Enterprise Standard and Plus, and Google AI Pro and Ultra tiers, requiring Workspace smart features to be enabled at the admin level rather than a separate procurement.

title: Google Meet now available on Android Auto
blurb: Google Meet is now available on Android Auto, letting users join and manage calls from a connected vehicle's dashboard without an admin control or separate setup. The feature is on by default for all Workspace customers, Individual subscribers, and personal account users, with scheduled-release domains completing rollout by June 26.

title: NY man charged after harassing college student with AI-generated nudes
blurb: A New York man, 21-year-old Anthony Belford, was indicted on a federal cyberstalking charge after allegedly using fake Instagram, LinkedIn, X, and Yahoo accounts to distribute AI-generated nude images of a Georgia college student and spread fabricated racist messages in her name. The case reinforces that federal law now covers AI-generated intimate imagery shared without consent, with the DOJ pointing victims to the FBI and the FTC's Take It Down platform for content removal.

title: Citrix now lets you run virtual desktops like a cost-conscious private equityeer
blurb: Citrix released DaaS Flex, a virtual-desktop offering that lets enterprises stream desktops to aging hardware and defer expensive PC refresh cycles. The pitch targets IT budgets squeezed by Windows 11 hardware requirements, positioning Citrix to capture endpoint-lifecycle spend that would otherwise go to Dell, HP, and Lenovo refresh orders.

title: Accenture Takes a Hit on Worsening Outlook and Cloudy AI Future
blurb: Accenture shares fell 18% after the CEO warned that revenue in coming months will come in below expectations, citing a worsening outlook and uncertainty over how AI reshapes IT consulting demand. The drop signals enterprise buyers are slowing discretionary consulting spend and rethinking large transformation contracts as AI tooling absorbs work that previously billed out to firms like Accenture.

title: Solving an ARD problem in AI: Agentic Resource Discovery
blurb: Google, Microsoft, Cisco, Nvidia, Salesforce and others proposed Agentic Resource Discovery (ARD), an open protocol that lets AI agents find and safely invoke the tools and services available inside a corporate domain. ARD adds a missing layer over siloed registries through published capability catalogs and crawling registries, giving enterprises a standard way to govern which tools agents reach without wiring every system together by hand.

title: Kubernetes-based virtualization proves its enterprise credentials on critical rail infrastructure
blurb: CSX, one of the largest U.S. freight railroads, standardized its on-premises virtualization on Red Hat OpenShift Virtualization with Portworx by Everpure, running safety-critical systems like Positive Train Control on Kubernetes after a head-to-head proof of concept. The move follows a three-year cloud migration of 80% of its workload and reflects enterprise pressure to escape single-vendor lock-in after recent virtualization-market consolidation.

title: Microsoft finally admits its default Windows 11 25H2, 24H2 action broke key legacy component
blurb: Microsoft acknowledged that its July 2025 switch to the JScript9Legacy engine, default-enabled on Windows 11 24H2 and 25H2, breaks older apps and web components by no longer persisting JScript global definitions and execution context across scripts. The fix-for-security tradeoff means enterprise IT teams running legacy line-of-business apps on these builds have likely hit silent compatibility failures for close to a year before the support article confirmed the cause.

title: IT operating models remain unfit for the AI era, CEOs say
blurb: Gartner reported that CEOs see traditional IT operating models as unfit for an era of pervasive AI and geopolitical volatility, citing frameworks built for stable, centralized technology delivery. The implication for CIOs is pressure to restructure around distributed AI ownership and faster procurement cycles, rather than the multi-year planning horizons most IT organizations still run on.

title: What CIOs should know about AI-driven SaaS pricing changes
blurb: SaaS vendors are restructuring AI pricing away from flat subscriptions toward usage- and outcome-based models, where customers pay per query, per agent action, or per resolved ticket. The shift complicates IT budgeting and procurement, since consumption-based costs are harder to forecast than seat licenses and can scale unpredictably as teams adopt AI features across the stack.

title: France’s OVHcloud bets on frontier AI as Europe seeks alternatives to US models
blurb: OVHcloud is moving beyond cloud infrastructure to train a family of frontier AI models from scratch, which it plans to open-source once they hit performance targets, positioning the French provider as a sovereign-cloud alternative to US labs and a domestic rival to Mistral. CEO Octave Klaba pegs training cost at under $230M, down from roughly $1.15B, though analysts note fine-tuning, sovereign infrastructure, and serving costs make sustained competitiveness the harder bet.

title: Apple CEO Tim Cook warns AI-driven price increases are unavoidable — says company is trying its best but 'the situation has become unsustainable'
blurb: Apple CEO Tim Cook told the Wall Street Journal that AI-driven memory cost increases have become unsustainable and price hikes across iPhone, Mac, and iPad are now unavoidable, though he declined to specify timing or magnitude. TechInsights estimates Apple would need to raise a flagship iPhone Pro by roughly $270 to hold current gross margins, as surging LPDDR and 3D NAND prices collide with its expanding on-device AI memory demands.

title: AI Catalog updates for governance and operations
blurb: GitLab 19.1 added event-driven triggers to its AI Catalog, letting Duo Flows fire automatically on pipeline failure, success, or cancellation, merge conflicts, and draft-to-ready transitions instead of requiring a manual mention or assignment in the UI. The release pairs those triggers with governance controls and config validation so enterprise admins can audit what AI workflows run in their environment and let automation operate continuously without human handoff.

title: Hostile states behind three-quarters of UK critical infrastructure attacks
blurb: The UK's NCSC handled more than 200 cyber incidents against critical national infrastructure over the past year, with roughly 75% linked to state actors, particularly Russia, China, and Iran. CEO Richard Horne warned that adversaries are prepositioning footholds for disruption during conflict and that frontier AI models are accelerating vulnerability discovery, putting most of the defensive burden on the private operators who hold this infrastructure.

title: Microsoft will soon allow some users to block Copilot from analyzing their Office files
blurb: Microsoft is updating a Purview sensitivity-label setting to block Copilot and other connected AI services from analyzing labeled Word, Excel, and PowerPoint files, enforcing the restriction across all connected experiences rather than exempting AI tools. The change, tagged MC1297982, applies only to commercial customers with a Purview license and rolls out in phases through the end of next month, with already-labeled files upgraded automatically.

title: Sources: PC makers, including HP, are in talks with their supply-chain partners about using CXMT's memory chips in products bound for Asia as DRAM prices soar (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: HP and other PC makers are in talks with supply-chain partners about using DRAM from China's CXMT in Asia-bound products, according to the Wall Street Journal, as memory prices surge. The move would diversify supply away from Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron, but routing CXMT parts only to Asian markets signals caution over US export rules and potential restrictions on the sanctioned-adjacent supplier.

title: US energy regulator moves to speed up data center projects
blurb: FERC unanimously approved orders requiring the six largest US interstate grid operators, which serve about 200 million people, to justify or revise their data center interconnection rules in order to cut the time needed to power on new facilities. The directives prioritize flexible large loads and behind-the-meter co-location, signaling to hyperscalers like Microsoft that regulatory friction on grid access is easing as AI buildout accelerates.

title: Windows 11 version 26H2 is now available for testing in the latest preview build
blurb: Microsoft moved the Windows 11 Experimental Channel (formerly Dev) to version 26H2 in this week's Insider preview builds 26300.8697 and 26220.8690, with no new features beyond the version bump. The release focuses on reliability fixes, including a reworked File Explorer copy dialog in dark mode, faster Start menu app updates, and a patch for HYPERVISOR_ERROR and KMODE_EXCEPTION bugchecks hitting some devices.

title: F5 Patches Two Critical NGINX Open Source Flaws Enabling Remote Code Execution
blurb: F5 patched two critical NGINX flaws, both rated CVSS 9.2, that let a remote unauthenticated attacker run code via the HTTP/3 QUIC module (CVE-2026-42530) or an HTTP/2 proxy heap overflow (CVE-2026-42055). The fixes land across NGINX Open Source, Plus, Ingress Controller, and Gateway Fabric, with stopgap mitigations to disable HTTP/3 or drop the ignore_invalid_headers off directive until teams upgrade.

title: Build your own vulnerability harness
blurb: Cloudflare's Project Glasswing team details how to build a model-agnostic vulnerability-scanning harness, arguing that generic coding agents fail because they hold one hypothesis at a time and lose state during context compaction. The architecture treats models as interchangeable, using one for discovery and a different one for validation so findings are cross-checked by distinct logic, then traces vulnerabilities across cross-repo dependencies down to a triaged queue.

title: Ubiquiti: Enterprise NAS, Built on ZFS
blurb: Ubiquiti introduced ENAS, an enterprise NAS platform built on ZFS that aims to deliver local, high-performance storage without the licensing fees, proprietary hardware, and management overhead of incumbent enterprise systems. The ZFS foundation brings snapshots, checksumming, and pooled storage to teams looking to cut storage spend, though it leaves the operational burden of running ZFS at scale on the buyer.

title: Announcing Amazon EC2 G7 instances accelerated by NVIDIA RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs
blurb: AWS made general availability for EC2 G7 instances, the first major cloud offering of NVIDIA RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs, delivering up to 4.6x AI inference and 2.1x graphics performance over G6. Each instance scales to 8 GPUs and 256GB of GPU memory, with 700 Gbps of EFA networking (7x G6) and 7.6TB of local NVMe to keep large models and datasets close to compute.

title: Caching for Dollars, Not Hits: An Exact Offline Reference for Cloud-Egress Caching and the Crossover That Decides When It Pays
blurb: This paper argues that classic cache eviction optimizes the wrong metric: with cloud object storage billed per GET and per byte of egress, miss rate is irrelevant and dollar cost is what matters. The authors build an exact offline dollar-optimal reference and show cost-aware GreedyDual cuts LRU's dollar-regret to roughly a tenth. A closed-form crossover, s* = GET_fee/egress_rate (about 4KB on S3, 330B on GCS), predicts which deployments need dollar-aware caching at all.

title: Tesco UK supermarket chain removes 40,000 servers from VMware infrastructure — mass exodus continues due to Broadcom's aggressive subscription model
blurb: Tesco is replacing VMware across its entire fleet of 40,000 servers by 2027, the largest publicly known migration off the platform since Broadcom's 2023 acquisition and a follow-on to the $134M lawsuit Tesco filed over voided perpetual licenses. The chain cites a 175% price hike on VMware and 350% on mainframe software, plus Broadcom cutting support and security updates for non-subscription customers.

title: GitLab 19.1 released
blurb: GitLab 19.1 shipped on June 18 with general availability for Secret false positive detection in the GitLab Duo Agent Platform, which auto-analyzes critical and high-severity secret findings after each scan and posts a confidence-scored true/false-positive assessment to the vulnerability report. The release also adds an always-on availability mode that lets admins lock GitLab Duo on across an instance or top-level group, blocking project owners from disabling it for centralized AI governance.

title: Novo Nordisk Breach Exposes Software Development Pipeline Risk
blurb: The author argues that the Novo Nordisk breach, traced to a leaked GitHub token, proves most teams scope secrets management wrong by treating it as a tooling problem instead of an identity one. The takeaway for platform teams: credentials in the development pipeline are machine identities that need rotation, scoped permissions, and revocation, not just a vault to hide them in. Static long-lived tokens remain the weakest link.

title: 2,000 retired Google Pixel phones get a second life as a private cloud
blurb: A team rebuilt 2,000 retired Google Pixel phones into a working private cloud, racking the handsets as low-power ARM nodes rather than e-wasting them. The writeup details the practical hurdles: building custom enclosures and cooling for consumer hardware, flashing a maintainable OS image across the fleet, and orchestrating nodes that ship with batteries and no server-grade remote management. The payoff is cheap, energy-efficient compute reclaimed from hardware most orgs would have recycled.
